# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Taxonomy of All Disasters (Comprehensive KTU Module 1 Framework)

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Classification adopted by NDMA, India and UNDRR (Sendai Framework)**

### **Category A — Natural Disasters**

Natural disasters originate from **geological, hydrological, meteorological, climatic, and biological** processes of the Earth.

#### **A.1 Geological / Geophysical Disasters**

These arise from tectonic, volcanic, or mass-movement processes of the Earth's crust.

| Sub-Type | Cause | Primary Effect | KTU Practical Example |
|----------|-------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Earthquake** | Sudden release of accumulated tectonic stress along a fault line | Ground shaking, surface rupture, liquefaction | 2001 Bhuj (Gujarat, India) – M 7.7; 2023 Turkey–Syria – M 7.8 |
| **Tsunami** | Underwater earthquake, volcanic eruption, or submarine landslide displacing water column | Coastal inundation, drowning, infrastructure washout | 2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami (Tamil Nadu, Sri Lanka, Indonesia) |
| **Volcanic Eruption** | Magma ascent through crust due to pressure release | Lava flow, ash fall, pyroclastic surge, lahars | 2018 Anak Krakatau (Indonesia) collapse-induced tsunami |
| **Landslide** | Saturation of soil, seismic shaking, deforestation, slope undercutting | Mass burial of settlements, dam formation | 2014 Malin (Maharashtra) landslide; 2023 Wayanad (Kerala) landslide |
| **Avalanche** | Snow instability on steep slopes triggered by temperature or human activity | Burial of ski resorts, villages | 2012 Gayari Sector (Pakistan) avalanche – 140 casualties |

#### **A.2 Hydro-Meteorological Disasters**

Caused by atmospheric, oceanic, or hydrological processes.

| Sub-Type | Cause | Primary Effect | KTU Practical Example |
|----------|-------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Floods** | Heavy rainfall, dam break, glacial lake outburst (GLOF), storm surge | Inundation of land, loss of crops, displacement | 2018 Kerala Floods (484 deaths); 2013 Uttarakhand floods |
| **Droughts** | Prolonged deficit of precipitation vs. evaporation | Crop failure, famine, water scarcity | 2016 Marathwada (Maharashtra) drought |
| **Cyclones / Hurricanes / Typhoons** | Low-pressure systems over warm ocean waters (>26.5°C) | High wind, storm surge, coastal flooding | 1999 Odisha Super Cyclone; 2023 Cyclone Biparjoy (Gujarat) |
| **Tornadoes** | Severe rotating convective storms (mesocyclones) | Localized extreme destruction | 2018 Kerala/Tamil Nadu localized tornadoes |
| **Lightning / Thunderstorms** | Atmospheric electrical discharge | Forest fires, deaths, infrastructure damage | Annual ~2,500 deaths in India (highest globally) |
| **Cloudburst** | Localized extreme precipitation (>100 mm/hour) | Flash floods, landslides | 2013 Kedarnath disaster; 2015 Chennai floods |
| **Heatwaves** | Sustained temperatures exceeding regional climatological norms | Heatstroke, mortality, infrastructure stress | 2015 Indian Heatwave (2,500+ deaths) |

#### **A.3 Climate-Driven Disasters**

| Sub-Type | Cause | Primary Effect | KTU Practical Example |
|----------|-------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Wildfires** | Lightning, arson, prolonged drought, climate change | Forest loss, air pollution, displacement | 2019–20 Australian Bushfires ("Black Summer") |
| **Cold Waves / Frost** | Extreme low temperatures, ice storms | Hypothermia, crop damage | 2023 North India cold wave |
| **Sea-Level Rise** | Thermal expansion + cryosphere melt | Coastal submergence, salinization | Sundarbans (West Bengal), Maldives |

#### **A.4 Biological Disasters**

| Sub-Type | Cause | Primary Effect | KTU Practical Example |
|----------|-------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Epidemics** | Pathogen outbreak (bacterial, viral) confined to a region | Disease spread, mortality | Nipah virus (Kerala, 2018, 2023) |
| **Pandemics** | Worldwide spread of a novel pathogen | Global health emergency | COVID-19 (SARS-CoV-2, 2020–2023) |
| **Pest Infestations** | Locust swarms, crop diseases | Agricultural destruction | 2019–20 Locust Crisis (Rajasthan, Gujarat) |

---

### **Category B — Man-Made (Anthropogenic) Disasters**

Disasters resulting from human actions, technological failure, or negligence.

#### **B.1 Technological / Industrial Disasters**

| Sub-Type | Cause | Primary Effect | KTU Practical Example |
|----------|-------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Chemical Industrial Disaster** | Leakage of toxic chemicals (MIC, chlorine, ammonia) | Toxic exposure, Bhopal-like gas tragedy | 1984 Bhopal Gas Tragedy (Union Carbide, 3,800+ deaths) |
| **Nuclear / Radiological Disaster** | Reactor meltdown, radiation release | Cancer, genetic mutation, evacuation | 1986 Chernobyl (USSR); 2011 Fukushima (Japan) |
| **Structural Collapse** | Engineering failure, poor maintenance | Building/bridge collapse | 2013 Thane Building Collapse; 2024 Jaipur stepwell collapse |
| **Mine Disasters** | Roof collapse, gas explosion, flooding in mines | Trapped workers, explosions | 2016 Meghalaya rat-hole mine flooding (15 trapped) |
| **Oil Spills / Pipeline Explosions** | Pipeline rupture, tanker accidents | Fire, marine pollution | 2010 Deepwater Horizon (Gulf of Mexico); GAIL pipeline blast, Andhra Pradesh (2014) |
| **Dam Failure** | Structural collapse, overtopping, foundation failure | Catastrophic downstream flooding | 1979 Machhu Dam (Gujarat, 2,000+ deaths) |

#### **B.2 Transportation Disasters**

| Sub-Type | Cause | Primary Effect | KTU Practical Example |
|----------|-------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Road Accidents** | Human error, poor roads, vehicle failure | Mass casualties, fires | Annual ~1.5 lakh deaths in India (MoRTH) |
| **Rail Accidents** | Derailment, collision, signal failure | Mass casualties | 2016 Indore-Patna Express derailment (150 deaths) |
| **Air Crashes** | Pilot error, mechanical failure, weather | Mass air disasters | 2010 Mangalore Air India Express crash (158 deaths) |
| **Maritime Accidents** | Sinking, collision, capsizing | Drowning, oil spill | 2023 Adani-owned MV Daiki Aida, Bay of Bengal |

#### **B.3 Societal / Civil Disasters**

| Sub-Type | Cause | Primary Effect | KTU Practical Example |
|----------|-------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Terrorism** | Politically/religiously motivated violence | Mass casualties, fear | 2008 Mumbai 26/11 attacks; 2019 Pulwama (CRPF convoy) |
| **Communal / Sectarian Riots** | Social tensions, hate propaganda | Mass violence, displacement | 2002 Gujarat riots; 2024 Manipur ethnic violence |
| **Stampede / Crowd Crush** | Poor crowd management, narrow exits | Trampling deaths | 2014 Kumbh Mela stampede (Nashik); 2022 Halloween crowd crush, Seoul (Itaewon) |
| **War / Armed Conflict** | Geopolitical conflict | Destruction, displacement, refugee crisis | Russia–Ukraine war (2022–ongoing); Israel–Gaza (2023–) |
| **Fire (Urban / Forest)** | Electrical fault, arson, carelessness | Property loss, smoke inhalation | 2023 Kurla slum fire (Mumbai); Uphaar tragedy (1997 Delhi) |

#### **B.4 Environmental / Anthropogenic Hazards**

| Sub-Type | Cause | Primary Effect | KTU Practical Example |
|----------|-------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Air Pollution** | Vehicular emissions, industrial smoke, stubble burning | Respiratory illness, smog | Delhi NCR AQI > 500 (Nov 2023) |
| **Water Pollution** | Industrial discharge, sewage | Disease, ecosystem damage | Yamuna pollution; Kerala's Endosulfan disaster (2011) |
| **Deforestation** | Logging, mining, urbanization | Soil erosion, biodiversity loss, landslides | Western Ghats deforestation triggering 2018 Kerala floods |
| **Soil Degradation** | Overuse of pesticides, salinization | Agricultural decline | Punjab & Haryana soil degradation |
| **Cyber Disasters** | Hacking, ransomware, infrastructure attack | Data loss, financial disruption | 2017 Wannacry ransomware; 2021 Colonial Pipeline (USA) |

---

### **Category C — Hybrid / Complex Emergencies**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid disasters** involve the **convergence of a natural trigger and a human-induced vulnerability**. They are the most complex and are a key focus of the **Sendai Framework 2015–2030**.

**Practical Example:** **2018 Kerala Floods** — Triggered by **natural** extreme rainfall (~42% excess), but the **severity was amplified by anthropogenic factors**: deforestation of the Western Ghats, encroachments on river floodplains, illegal quarrying, and unscientific dam water release.

**Practical Example:** **COVID-19 Pandemic** — A **biological** origin (natural virus), but the **severity was amplified by global travel networks**, dense urbanization, and inadequate public health infrastructure.

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examined in KTU Module 1. Memorize the disaster risk equation, magnitude scales, and return-period concepts.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **UNDRR Disaster Risk Equation** | $R = H \times V \times E / C$ | H = Hazard probability, V = Vulnerability (0–1), E = Exposure (people/assets), C = Capacity (mitigation/response) | Backbone of all KTU disaster management planning |
| 2 | **Richter / Moment Magnitude (Earthquake Energy Release)** | $\log_{10} E = 1.5 M + 4.8$ | E = Energy in Joules, M = Magnitude | Used in seismic design of buildings |
| 3 | **Modified Mercalli Intensity (MMI)** | MMI = I to XII (qualitative) | I = Not felt, XII = Total destruction | Damage assessment scale |
| 4 | **Beaufort Wind Scale** | $v = 0.836 \times B^{3/2}$ (m/s) | v = wind speed, B = Beaufort number | Cyclone classification |
| 5 | **Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale** | Category 1 to 5 | Based on sustained wind speed | Coastal evacuation planning |
| 6 | **Flood Return Period (T)** | $T = 1 / p$ | p = annual exceedance probability | Dam and bridge design |
| 7 | **Vulnerability Index (VI)** | $VI = f(C, S, E)$ | C = Coping, S = Sensitivity, E = Exposure | KTU Module 2 and 3 carry-forward |
| 8 | **Fire Triangle** | $Fuel + Heat + Oxygen \rightarrow Fire$ | For fire management & suppression | Fire engineering |
| 9 | **Fire Tetrahedron** | Adds $Chain\ Reaction$ to the Fire Triangle | For modern fire dynamics | Industrial fire safety |
| 10 | **Vulnerability Reduction Factor (VRF)** | $VRF = (V_{before} - V_{after}) / V_{before}$ | Measure of risk mitigation success | Used in NDMP (India) |

---

## 2.3 Universal Causal Mechanism — Why Disasters Happen

> [!TIP]
> **The KTU "3 Cs" Causal Framework for any disaster:**
>
> 1. **Cause (C₁)** — The triggering physical/chemical/biological event.
> 2. **Condition (C₂)** — Pre-existing vulnerability of the community (poverty, weak infrastructure, urbanization).
> 3. **Capacity Failure (C₃)** — Inadequate early warning, preparedness, response, and recovery systems.
>
> **If any one of C₁, C₂, C₃ is mitigated, disaster severity drops drastically.** This is the *philosophical backbone* of disaster risk reduction.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Engineers MUST study Disaster Causes and Effects:**
>
> - **Civil Engineers** — Earthquake-resistant design (IS 1893), flood-resistant structures, landslide mitigation.
> - **Mechanical Engineers** — Industrial safety systems (pressure relief valves), explosion-proof equipment.
> - **Electrical Engineers** — Lightning arresters, grid resilience against cyclones.
> - **Chemical Engineers** — Process safety management, HAZOP studies to prevent Bhopal-like tragedies.
> - **Environmental Engineers** — Air/water pollution monitoring, climate adaptation.
>
> **Every KTU engineering discipline intersects with disaster management.**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Disaster Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: UNDRR Disaster Risk Equation (Conceptual Derivation)

The **Disaster Risk Equation** is the analytical foundation of Module 1.

### **Step 1: Define the three primary input variables**

$$
\text{Let } H = \text{Probability of a hazardous event occurring at a given location in a given time}
$$

$$
\text{Let } V = \text{Intrinsic susceptibility of exposed elements to suffer damage (0 to 1)}
$$

$$
\text{Let } E = \text{Number of people, assets, or systems located in the hazard zone}
$$

### **Step 2: Define the mitigating variable (capacity)**

$$
\text{Let } C = \text{Sum of all strengths, attributes, and resources available to manage risk}
$$

### **Step 3: Construct the proportional relationship**

Disaster risk is **directly proportional** to hazard, vulnerability, and exposure, and **inversely proportional** to capacity:

$$
R \propto H \cdot V \cdot E
$$

$$
R \propto \frac{1}{C}
$$

### **Step 4: Combine into the canonical UNDRR equation**

$$
R = \frac{H \times V \times E}{C}
$$

### **Step 5: Interpretation for engineering**

If we reduce $V$ by **50%** (e.g., earthquake-resistant retrofitting) and $C$ is doubled (early warning + trained rescue), the new risk becomes:

$$
R_{new} = \frac{H \times 0.5 V \times E}{2 C} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot R_{original}
$$

> **Result: A 75% reduction in disaster risk.** This is the analytical justification for the **Hyogo Framework (2005–2015)** and the **Sendai Framework (2015–2030)** priorities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Earthquake Energy Released (Gutenberg–Richter Law)

The relationship between earthquake magnitude (M) and released energy (E in Joules):

$$
\log_{10} E = 1.5 M + 4.8
$$

### **Step 1: Solve for E**

$$
E = 10^{1.5M + 4.8}
$$

### **Step 2: Calculate energy difference between M 7 and M 8**

$$
\frac{E_{M8}}{E_{M7}} = \frac{10^{1.5 \times 8 + 4.8}}{10^{1.5 \times 7 + 4.8}}
$$

$$
= \frac{10^{16.8}}{10^{15.3}}
$$

$$
= 10^{16.8 - 15.3} = 10^{1.5}
$$

$$
= 31.62
$$

> **Conclusion:** A **one-unit increase in magnitude releases ~31.6 times more energy.** The 2023 Turkey–Syria M 7.8 earthquake released 31.6× more energy than a M 6.8 quake.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Flood Return Period Concept

For hydrological design, the **return period (T)** in years is given by:

$$
T = \frac{1}{p}
$$

Where $p$ = probability of exceedance in any given year.

### **Step 1: A 100-year flood has probability**

$$
p = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01 = 1\%
$$

### **Step 2: Over a 30-year period (typical building lifetime), probability of at least one exceedance**

$$
P(\text{at least one flood in 30 years}) = 1 - (1 - p)^n
$$

$$
= 1 - (1 - 0.01)^{30}
$$

$$
= 1 - (0.99)^{30}
$$

$$
= 1 - 0.7397 = 0.2603 \approx 26\%
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A "100-year flood" has a **26% chance of occurring at least once in 30 years.** This refutes the common myth of safety from rare events.

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Disaster Impact Matrix (Master Reference for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Lives Lost – Economy – Environment" Trinity — Master Matrix for All Disasters**

| Disaster Type | Lives Lost (Typical) | Economic Loss | Environmental Impact | Societal Impact | Mitigation Strategy |
|---------------|----------------------|---------------|---------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Earthquake** | Hundreds to hundreds of thousands (Bhuj: 20,000+; Haiti 2010: 230,000+) | Billions of USD | Liquefaction, landslides | Mass displacement | Seismic design (IS 1893), retrofitting |
| **Tsunami** | Tens of thousands (2004: 230,000) | Billions USD | Coastal erosion, salinity | Coastal community collapse | Early warning (IOTWS), mangrove restoration |
| **Flood** | Thousands/year globally | $50–100 billion/year | Water pollution, soil erosion | Crop failure, displacement | Floodplain zoning, dams, levees |
| **Cyclone** | Hundreds to thousands | Billions USD | Storm surge, coastal damage | Coastal livelihoods destroyed | Coastal shelters, evacuation, mangroves |
| **Drought** | Slow onset, food insecurity | $10–20 billion/year | Desertification, water table drop | Famine, migration | Watershed mgmt, drought-resistant crops |
| **Volcanic Eruption** | Local but severe (villages buried) | Aviation loss (ash clouds) | LAsh, gas emissions | Long displacement | Exclusion zones, evacuation planning |
| **Industrial Disaster** | Hundreds (Bhopal: 3,800+) | Billions USD | Toxic contamination | Cancer clusters | Process safety (PSM), HAZOP, land-use |
| **Nuclear Accident** | Long-term (Chernobyl: 4,000–93,000 long-term) | Trillions USD | Radioactive contamination | Permanent evacuation | Containment, safety culture |
| **Pandemic** | Millions (1918 Flu: 50M; COVID-19: 7M+) | Trillions USD | Disrupted ecosystems | Lockdowns, mental health | Surveillance, vaccination, PPE |
| **Terrorism** | Tens to hundreds per incident | Billions USD | Long-term fear | Policy overhaul | Intelligence, infrastructure hardening |
| **Stampede** | Hundreds (Itaewon: 159) | Localized | Minimal | Public panic | Crowd management, exit planning |
| **Fire (urban)** | Tens to hundreds | Billions USD | Air pollution, toxic smoke | Displacement | Building codes, sprinklers, fire exits |

## 3.5 Causal Chain Analysis — A Universal Framework

> Every disaster follows a **Causal Chain**. KTU expects students to map these chains.

**Generic Causal Chain (Universal Template):**

$$
\text{Triggers} \rightarrow \text{Hazard Event} \rightarrow \text{Primary Damage} \rightarrow \text{Secondary Damage} \rightarrow \text{Disruption} \rightarrow \text{Recovery / Failure}
$$

### **Worked Example 1: Earthquake**

$$
\text{Tectonic Stress Buildup} \rightarrow \text{Fault Rupture (Hazard)} \rightarrow \text{Ground Shaking (Primary)} \rightarrow \text{Building Collapse, Fires (Secondary)} \rightarrow \text{Economic / Health System Breakdown} \rightarrow \text{Reconstruction Phase}
$$

### **Worked Example 2: Bhopal Gas Tragedy**

$$
\text{Storage of MIC in Tank 610} \rightarrow \text{Water ingress, runaway reaction} \rightarrow \text{MIC gas release (Hazard)} \rightarrow \text{Toxic cloud over slums (Primary)} \rightarrow \text{Pulmonary edema, blindness (Secondary)} \rightarrow \text{Long-term genetic disorders (Tertiary)}
$$

### **Worked Example 3: 2018 Kerala Floods (Hybrid)**

$$
\text{35\% excess SW monsoon} \rightarrow \text{Landslide dam bursts + reservoir overtopping} \rightarrow \text{Flash floods in 13 districts} \rightarrow \text{341 deaths, 1.4M displaced} \rightarrow \text{Economic loss ₹40,000 crore}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a "disaster" as per the UNDRR. Differentiate between a hazard and a disaster.**

#### **Model Answer:**

> According to the **United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR)**, a **disaster** is *"a serious disruption of the functioning of a community or a society involving widespread human, material, economic or environmental losses and impacts, which exceeds the community's or society's ability to cope using its own resources."*
>
> **Difference between Hazard and Disaster:**
>
> | Aspect | Hazard | Disaster |
> |--------|--------|----------|
> | Definition | A dangerous phenomenon or condition that may cause harm | Actual realization of a hazard impacting a vulnerable community |
> | Realization | Potential / latent | Actual / realized |
> | Dependency | Independent of human presence | Depends on human exposure and vulnerability |
>
> **Example:** An earthquake of magnitude 7 is a *hazard*. When it strikes a populated city and causes fatalities and infrastructure damage, it becomes a *disaster*.
>
> **Disaster Risk Equation:** $R = H \times V \times E \, / \, C$

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> - Students **commonly lose 3–5 marks** by **failing to classify disasters** (Natural vs Man-made vs Hybrid) before describing them. Always start with classification.
> - Avoid **listing effects without structure**. Use the **Primary → Secondary → Tertiary** framework.
> - **Never skip citing real practical examples** — examiners award at least 2–3 marks for verified, named examples (e.g., 1984 Bhopal, 2004 Tsunami).
> - Do **not confuse "hazard" with "disaster"** in definitions — this is the #1 trap in Module 1.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** Classify natural disasters in detail with suitable practical examples. Highlight the geological and hydro-meteorological categories. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Discuss the causes, effects, and lessons learned from the **1984 Bhopal Gas Tragedy** and the **2011 Fukushima Nuclear Disaster**. Compare their long-term environmental impacts. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer A(a):**

**Classification of Natural Disasters (7 Marks)**

Natural disasters are adverse events triggered by **natural Earth processes**, classified into **four major categories** by the UNDRR and NDMA:

**1. Geological / Geophysical Disasters** (Origin: Earth's interior)
   - **Earthquake:** Sudden release of tectonic stress along fault lines. *Example:* 2001 Bhuj earthquake (M 7.7, 20,000+ deaths); 2023 Turkey-Syria earthquake (M 7.8, 50,000+ deaths).
   - **Tsunami:** Long-wavelength oceanic waves generated by underwater earthquakes. *Example:* 2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami (230,000+ deaths across 14 countries).
   - **Volcanic Eruption:** Release of magma, ash, and gases. *Example:* Mount Pinatubo (1991, Philippines); Anak Krakatau collapse (2018).
   - **Landslides:** Mass movement of rock/debris on slopes. *Example:* 2014 Malin (Maharashtra); 2023 Wayanad (Kerala).
   - **Avalanches:** Snow mass slides. *Example:* 2012 Gayari sector (Pakistan, 140 deaths).

**2. Hydro-Meteorological Disasters** (Origin: Atmosphere, ocean, hydrology)
   - **Floods:** Most common globally; cause ~7,000 deaths/year. *Example:* 2018 Kerala floods (341 deaths, ₹40,000 crore loss).
   - **Cyclones / Hurricanes:** Low-pressure systems over warm oceans. *Example:* 1999 Odisha Super Cyclone (10,000+ deaths); 2023 Biparjoy.
   - **Droughts:** Prolonged precipitation deficit. *Example:* 2016 Marathwada drought.
   - **Heatwaves:** Extreme sustained temperatures. *Example:* 2015 Indian heatwave (2,500+ deaths).
   - **Tornadoes / Lightning / Cloudbursts.**

**3. Climate-Driven Disasters:** Wildfires (Australia 2019–20), cold waves, sea-level rise (Sundarbans).

**4. Biological Disasters:** Epidemics (Nipah, Kerala 2018); pandemics (COVID-19); pest outbreaks (Locust, 2019).

> **Energy released by earthquake (Gutenberg–Richter):** $E = 10^{1.5M+4.8}$ Joules

---

#### **Model Answer A(b):**

**1984 Bhopal Gas Tragedy vs. 2011 Fukushima Nuclear Disaster (7 Marks)**

| Aspect | Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984) | Fukushima Nuclear Disaster (2011) |
|--------|---------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Date & Location** | 2–3 Dec 1984, Madhya Pradesh, India | 11 Mar 2011, Fukushima, Japan |
| **Type** | Man-made chemical industrial disaster | Hybrid (natural trigger + technological failure) |
| **Cause** | Water ingress into MIC (Methyl Isocyanate) Tank 610 caused runaway exothermic reaction, releasing 40 tons of toxic MIC gas | M 9.0 Tōhoku earthquake triggered 15-m tsunami that disabled diesel backup generators at Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant, causing core meltdown in 3 reactors |
| **Immediate Deaths** | 3,800+ immediate deaths; ~15,000–25,000 premature deaths in subsequent years | Zero immediate radiation deaths, but long-term cancer risk |
| **Affected Population** | 500,000+ exposed; 100,000+ permanently disabled | 154,000 evacuated from 30-km exclusion zone |
| **Environmental Impact** | Persistent soil and groundwater contamination by MIC residues; lasting genetic disorders | Radioactive contamination of soil, groundwater, and Pacific Ocean (300+ tonnes of radioactive water); cesium-137 in seafood |
| **Long-Term Effects** | Birth defects, chronic respiratory illness, soil toxicity persists 40 years later | Permanent exclusion zone, fisheries destroyed, distrust in nuclear energy globally |
| **Lessons Learned** | Process Safety Management (PSM); HAZOP studies; industrial siting away from habitations; community right-to-know laws (Bhopal Act 1985) | Multiple-redundancy safety systems; seawall design; hardened emergency cooling (BWR Mark I containment flaws revealed); led to global nuclear pause |
| **Economic Loss** | ~$500 million immediate (1984 USD); multi-billion in compensation claims | ~$200+ billion; TEPCO bankruptcy; Japan halted nuclear program for years |

> **Comparison Insight:** Bhopal remains the **world's worst industrial disaster**; Fukushima is the **second-worst nuclear disaster after Chernobyl**. Both demonstrate the catastrophic potential when **technological systems fail without adequate redundancy** in a vulnerable populated environment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**a)** Discuss the causes, primary, secondary, and tertiary effects of **earthquakes** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Describe the causes and effects of **climate-induced disasters** (floods, droughts, cyclones, and wildfires) with practical examples from India. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer B(a):**

**Earthquakes — Causes, Effects, and Examples (7 Marks)**

**Causes:**
- **Tectonic plate movements:** Most earthquakes occur at plate boundaries (convergent, divergent, transform). 90% of earthquakes occur along the **Pacific Ring of Fire**.
- **Stress accumulation and sudden release along fault lines** (elastic rebound theory by H.F. Reid).
- **Volcanic activity:** Local earthquakes accompany magma movement.
- **Induced seismicity:** Reservoir-induced (Koyna 1967, M 6.6), fracking, mining.

**Gutenberg-Richter Relation:** Energy released $E = 10^{1.5M + 4.8}$ Joules. A one-unit magnitude increase releases ~31.6× more energy.

**Effects:**

**Primary Effects (Direct):**
- Ground shaking → building collapse (e.g., 2001 Bhuj)
- Surface fault rupture
- Liquefaction (water-saturated soil loses strength)
- Landslides triggered by shaking (e.g., 2015 Nepal earthquake triggered Mt. Everest avalanches)

**Secondary Effects (Indirect):**
- Fires (1906 San Francisco earthquake destroyed 80% of city due to fire)
- Tsunamis (2004 Indian Ocean, 2011 Tōhoku)
- Epidemics in displacement camps
- Dam failures (e.g., aftershocks can damage dams)

**Tertiary Effects (Long-Term):**
- Economic recession (Haiti 2010, $7.8 billion loss — 120% of GDP)
- Mental health crisis (PTSD, depression)
- Demographic shifts (rural-to-urban migration)
- Policy reform (building codes like IS 1893:2016)

**Indian Examples:**
- **2001 Bhuj (Gujarat):** M 7.7, 20,000+ deaths, ₹200,000 crore loss
- **2015 Nepal (impacted India):** M 7.8, 9,000 deaths, aftershock at M 7.3
- **2023 Turkey-Syria:** M 7.8 + M 7.5 doublet, 50,000+ deaths

> **Engineering Mitigation:** Base isolation, shear walls, moment-resistant frames, ductile detailing (IS 13920:2016).

---

#### **Model Answer B(b):**

**Climate-Induced Disasters (7 Marks)**

| Disaster | Causes | Primary Effects | Indian Example |
|----------|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Floods** | Heavy monsoon rainfall, cloudbursts, dam breaks, GLOF, climate change intensifying hydrological cycle | Inundation, drowning, crop loss, infrastructure damage | 2018 Kerala Floods (341 deaths, ₹40,000 crore); 2013 Uttarakhand (5,700 deaths) |
| **Droughts** | Prolonged rainfall deficit, El Niño, deforestation, over-extraction of groundwater | Crop failure, drinking water scarcity, famine, farmer suicides | 2016 Marathwada (Maharashtra); 2002 Rajasthan drought |
| **Cyclones** | Warm sea surface (>26.5°C), low-pressure systems, climate change raising SST | High winds (>200 km/h), storm surge, coastal flooding | 1999 Odisha Super Cyclone (10,000+ deaths); 2021 Tauktae (Gujarat); 2023 Biparjoy |
| **Wildfires** | Lightning, arson, prolonged heat/drought, forest debris accumulation | Forest loss, biodiversity loss, air pollution (PM2.5), displacement | 2016 Uttarakhand forest fires (2,500 hectares); 2020 Bandipur Tiger Reserve fires |

**Climate Change as a Multiplier (UNDRR):** Climate change is **not a disaster itself but a "hazard multiplier"** — increasing the **frequency and intensity** of all hydro-meteorological disasters.

**India's Vulnerability:** 
- India's **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP, 2019)** identifies **12 out of 36 states/UTs as multi-hazard prone**.
- India ranks **7th globally** on the **Climate Risk Index (Germanwatch 2021)**.
- Approximately **80% of India's population lives in districts vulnerable to climate disasters**.

**Mitigation Strategies:**
- **Floods:** Floodplain zoning, watershed management, embankments, Early Warning Systems (CWC).
- **Droughts:** Watershed programs (PMKSY), drought-resistant crops, drip irrigation.
- **Cyclones:** India's **early warning system (IMD)** has reduced cyclone mortality by 95% over 50 years — a global success story.
- **Wildfires:** Forest Fire Prevention and Management Scheme (MoEFCC), community-based monitoring.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 1 Revision — Master 15 Bullets**

- [x] **Disaster Risk Equation:** $R = H \times V \times E \, / \, C$ — backbone of all answers.
- [x] **Hazard ≠ Disaster** — Hazard is potential; disaster is realized.
- [x] **Three Categories:** Natural / Man-made / Hybrid (Sendai Framework).
- [x] **Natural disasters** = Geological + Hydro-meteorological + Climate + Biological.
- [x] **Man-made disasters** = Technological + Transportation + Societal + Environmental.
- [x] **Hybrid disasters** = Natural trigger + human vulnerability amplification (2018 Kerala floods, COVID-19).
- [x] **Effects hierarchy:** Primary (direct) → Secondary (cascade) → Tertiary (long-term).
- [x] **Earthquake energy:** $E = 10^{1.5M + 4.8}$ J; 1-unit increase = **31.6× more energy**.
- [x] **Gutenberg-Richter Law** must be memorized for earthquake questions.
- [x] **Flood return period:** $T = 1/p$; a "100-year flood" has 26% chance in 30 years.
- [x] **Beaufort & Saffir-Simpson scales** for cyclone classification.
- [x] **Fire Triangle** (Fuel + Heat + O₂) and **Fire Tetrahedron** (adds chain reaction).
- [x] **1984 Bhopal** = worst industrial disaster globally; **2011 Fukushima** = 2nd worst nuclear disaster.
- [x] **Sendai Framework 2015–2030** priorities: (1) Understanding risk, (2) Strengthening governance, (3) Investing in resilience, (4) Enhancing preparedness.
- [x] **Always cite real examples** — 2004 Tsunami, 2001 Bhuj, 2018 Kerala, 2019–20 Australian fires, COVID-19 — examiners reward verified case studies with 2–3 bonus marks.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Disasters are not natural — hazards are natural. **Disasters are the failure of humans to manage natural hazards through preparedness, vulnerability reduction, and capacity building.***
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk and Vulnerability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT744 - DISASTER PREPAREDNESS AND EMERGENCY PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Risk and Vulnerability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1: Risk and Vulnerability Analysis

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (UNDRR-Aligned Terminology):**
> **Risk** in disaster management is defined as the *probability of harmful consequences, or expected losses (deaths, injuries, property, livelihoods, economic activity disrupted, or environment damaged)* resulting from interactions between **hazards**, **vulnerability**, and **capacity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulnerability** is formally defined as the *conditions determined by physical, social, economic, and environmental factors or processes which increase the susceptibility of an individual, a community, assets, or systems to the impacts of hazards.*

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of your **house** standing in a coastal village.

- A **cyclone** (hazard) hits every monsoon — that's the *threat*.
- Your house is built of **mud and thatch** (fragile material) and located **right on the shoreline** (exposed location) — that's *vulnerability*.
- If you have **no cyclone shutters, no insurance, and no early-warning phone** — that's *low capacity*.
- Now multiply all three: **Hazard × Vulnerability ÷ Capacity = Risk**.

👉 **Low risk** = strong house + good warning system + cyclone shutters, even though hazard is same.
👉 **High risk** = weak house + exposed location + no preparedness, even if cyclone is weaker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Highlight — The Risk Equation:**
> Risk is **NOT** just "danger." It is the *interaction* between hazard and the social/physical conditions of the people or assets exposed. Two cities hit by the same earthquake can have completely different disaster outcomes depending purely on their vulnerability profile.

### 📌 Standard Metrics (Examinable)

- **Probability (P):** Likelihood of hazard occurring, expressed as annual exceedance probability or return period.
- **Consequence (C):** Magnitude of expected loss (lives, ₹ value, downtime).
- **Vulnerability Index (Vᵢ):** Normalized 0 → 1 score.
- **Risk Score (R):** Often mapped to a qualitative matrix: **Low → Moderate → High → Extreme**.
- **Units:** Probability is dimensionless (0–1); Consequence in ₹ / fatalities / hectares; Risk often expressed in ₹/year (Annual Loss Expectancy, ALE).

---

## 1.2 Quick Terminology Snapshot (Board-Exam Favorite)

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard** | A dangerous event/phenomenon | Earthquake, flood, cyclone |
| **Exposure** | People/assets present in hazard zone | Population in floodplain |
| **Vulnerability** | Susceptibility to suffer harm | Old buildings, poor drainage |
| **Capacity** | Strength to cope/resist | Early warning, training, funds |
| **Risk** | Probability × Consequence | Expected annual loss |
| **Resilience** | Ability to bounce back | Recovery time, adaptive systems |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Risk — A Layered Concept

Risk in disaster management is a **multi-dimensional construct**. KTU examiners expect students to describe it as having **three core building blocks**:

### 🔹 Layer 1: Hazard (The Trigger)
- A *physical event* (natural or human-induced) that has the potential to cause harm.
- Characterized by **magnitude**, **frequency**, **duration**, **spatial extent**, and **onset speed**.
- Examples: Earthquake (M6.5), Cyclone (Category 3), Flood (100-year return period).

### 🔹 Layer 2: Exposure (What's in Harm's Way)
- The *inventory* of people, livelihoods, environmental services, infrastructure, and economic assets located in hazard-prone zones.
- **Exposure is a function of land-use planning and population distribution.**
- Example: 50,000 people living in a landslide-prone hill slope.

### 🔹 Layer 3: Vulnerability (How Fragile Are They?)
- The **propensity** of exposed elements to suffer adverse effects when a hazard event strikes.
- Composed of three sub-components:
  - **Physical vulnerability** — fragile buildings, weak infrastructure.
  - **Social vulnerability** — age (elderly/children), gender (women-headed households), poverty, literacy.
  - **Economic vulnerability** — livelihood dependence on climate-sensitive sectors (agriculture, fishing).

### 🔹 Layer 4: Capacity (The Counter-Force)
- The combination of **strengths, attributes, and resources** available to manage and reduce disaster risks.
- Includes coping capacity (short-term response) and adaptive capacity (long-term transformation).

## 2.2 The Canonical Risk Equation

The KTU 2024 syllabus (UNDRR / Sendai Framework-aligned) expresses risk as:

$$R = f(H, V, E)$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk
- $H$ = Hazard
- $V$ = Vulnerability
- $E$ = Exposure

More commonly decomposed as:

$$R = H \times V \times E$$

But with **capacity (C)** acting as a *divisor*, the **residual risk** is:

$$R_{residual} = \frac{H \times V \times E}{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "÷C"?** Because capacity *reduces* the effective vulnerability. A community with strong early-warning systems and earthquake-resistant buildings is *less vulnerable* than its physical characteristics alone suggest.

## 2.3 Vulnerability Decomposition (IPCC Framework)

For climate-related hazards, the IPCC defines vulnerability as:

$$V = f(E, S, AC)$$

Where:
- $E$ = Exposure
- $S$ = Sensitivity (degree to which the system is affected by climatic stimuli)
- $AC$ = Adaptive Capacity

Linear additive form (commonly used in KTU numericals):

$$V = (E + S) - AC$$

Or, in normalized form (0 to 1):

$$V_{index} = \frac{E + S}{2} - AC$$

Where all three variables are normalized to $[0, 1]$.

## 2.4 Quantitative Risk Formulas

### 🔸 Expected Annual Loss (EAL) — Most Tested in Board Exams

$$EAL = P \times C$$

Where:
- $P$ = Annual exceedance probability of the hazard
- $C$ = Consequence (loss value)

> **Generalized (for multiple hazard intensities):**

$$EAL = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times C_i$$

### 🔸 Risk Matrix Score (Qualitative Standard)

$$R_{score} = L \times S$$

Where:
- $L$ = Likelihood (1 to 5 scale)
- $S$ = Severity (1 to 5 scale)
- Resulting $R_{score}$ range: **1 (Low) to 25 (Extreme)**

### 🔸 Vulnerability Index — Weighted Multi-Indicator

$$V_{index} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot x_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j}$$

Where:
- $w_j$ = Weight assigned to indicator $j$
- $x_j$ = Normalized score of indicator $j$ (0 to 1)
- $m$ = Number of indicators

## 2.5 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = H \times V \times E$ | Base risk equation | Composite |
| 2 | $R_{residual} = \dfrac{H \times V \times E}{C}$ | Residual risk after capacity | Composite |
| 3 | $R = P \times C$ | Simple risk (probability × consequence) | ₹/year, lives/yr |
| 4 | $EAL = \sum P_i C_i$ | Expected annual loss | ₹/year |
| 5 | $R_{score} = L \times S$ | Qualitative risk matrix score | Dimensionless (1–25) |
| 6 | $V = (E + S) - AC$ | Vulnerability decomposition | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{index} = \dfrac{\sum w_j x_j}{\sum w_j}$ | Weighted vulnerability index | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | $ALE = P \times SLE$ | Annual Loss Expectancy in IT-security analogy | ₹/year |
| 9 | $C_{total} = C_{direct} + C_{indirect} + C_{intangible}$ | Total consequence (life + property + recovery) | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** In the formula $R = H \times V \times E$, students often write it as **R = H + V + E**. The correct relationship is **multiplicative**, because if *any* factor is **zero**, the *total risk* is **zero**. (No exposure → no risk, even if hazard is catastrophic.)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Risk Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Dam break analysis, floodplain mapping, building code design |
| **Structural Engineering** | Seismic design loads using $R = P \times C$ principles |
| **Urban Planning** | Land-use zoning based on vulnerability indexing |
| **Insurance** | Premium calculation using EAL |
| **Public Health** | Pandemic preparedness resource allocation |
| **Climate Adaptation** | IPCC vulnerability assessments for coastal districts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From "Hazard × Consequence" to Modern Risk Equation

### 🔸 Historical Evolution (Board Essay Favorite)

**Stage 1 — Classical Engineering View (1950s–1980s):**

Initially, engineers modelled risk as:

$$R_{classical} = P \times C$$

This worked well for *structural* risk (e.g., dam failure, bridge collapse), where the exposed system was well-defined and the consequence measurable in monetary terms.

**Stage 2 — Recognition of Social Dimensions (1990s):**

The 1994 Yokohama Conference and UNDRR's earlier *Yokohama Strategy* introduced **vulnerability** as a separate axis because disasters were disproportionately affecting poor populations even when physical hazards were identical. This gave:

$$R = P \times V \times C$$

**Stage 3 — Modern UNDRR/Sendai Framework (2015 onwards):**

Current KTU-syllabus formulation:

$$\boxed{R = f(H, V, E, C)}$$

Where $C$ in the denominator reflects **capacity** (adaptive + coping). The functional form is multiplicative:

$$R = \frac{H \cdot V \cdot E}{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiplicative?** If any single factor is zero, the overall risk must be zero. (If there is no exposure, there can be no disaster, regardless of hazard intensity.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Vulnerability Index from Raw Indicators

### 🔸 Problem Setup

A coastal panchayat wants to compute a vulnerability index using 4 indicators. Each indicator is scored 0 (best) to 1 (worst).

| Indicator ($j$) | Raw Score ($x_j$) | Weight ($w_j$) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Building fragility | 0.7 | 0.30 |
| 2. Poverty rate | 0.6 | 0.25 |
| 3. Early-warning coverage | 0.4 | 0.25 |
| 4. Distance from coast | 0.8 | 0.20 |

### 🔸 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1:** Compute numerator $\sum w_j x_j$

$$\sum w_j x_j = (0.30 \times 0.7) + (0.25 \times 0.6) + (0.25 \times 0.4) + (0.20 \times 0.8)$$

Computing each term:

$$0.30 \times 0.7 = 0.21$$

$$0.25 \times 0.6 = 0.15$$

$$0.25 \times 0.4 = 0.10$$

$$0.20 \times 0.8 = 0.16$$

Summing:

$$\sum w_j x_j = 0.21 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.16 = 0.62$$

**Step 2:** Compute denominator $\sum w_j$

$$\sum w_j = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 1.00$$

**Step 3:** Apply formula

$$V_{index} = \frac{0.62}{1.00} = 0.62$$

**Step 4:** Interpret

| $V_{index}$ Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| 0.00 – 0.20 | Very Low Vulnerability |
| 0.21 – 0.40 | Low Vulnerability |
| 0.41 – 0.60 | Moderate Vulnerability |
| 0.61 – 0.80 | High Vulnerability |
| 0.81 – 1.00 | Very High Vulnerability |

✅ **Result:** The panchayat has a **HIGH VULNERABILITY** index of 0.62.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Expected Annual Loss (EAL) Computation

### 🔸 Problem Statement

A coastal district faces cyclone hazards. Historical cyclone return periods and associated damage estimates are:

| Return Period (years) | Annual Probability $P_i$ | Consequence $C_i$ (₹ crore) |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | 0.10 | 50 |
| 50 | 0.02 | 250 |
| 100 | 0.01 | 500 |
| 500 | 0.002 | 1500 |

### 🔸 Calculation Using $EAL = \sum P_i \times C_i$

**Step 1:** For each return period, compute $P_i \times C_i$:

$$P_{10} \times C_{10} = 0.10 \times 50 = 5.0 \text{ crore/yr}$$

$$P_{50} \times C_{50} = 0.02 \times 250 = 5.0 \text{ crore/yr}$$

$$P_{100} \times C_{100} = 0.01 \times 500 = 5.0 \text{ crore/yr}$$

$$P_{500} \times C_{500} = 0.002 \times 1500 = 3.0 \text{ crore/yr}$$

**Step 2:** Sum all contributions:

$$EAL = 5.0 + 5.0 + 5.0 + 3.0 = 18.0 \text{ crore/year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Even though a Category-5 cyclone (500-yr event) causes ₹1500 crore damage, its *annual* contribution (₹3 cr/yr) is small because it occurs rarely. Conversely, frequent smaller cyclones contribute equally to the long-term annual loss budget.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Risk Matrix Score Classification

A landslide zone has:
- **Likelihood (L) = 4** (Likely)
- **Severity (S) = 5** (Catastrophic)

**Step 1:** Apply the risk matrix formula:

$$R_{score} = L \times S = 4 \times 5 = 20$$

**Step 2:** Classify using the standard 5×5 matrix:

| $R_{score}$ | Classification |
|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | **Low** |
| 5 – 9 | **Moderate** |
| 10 – 15 | **High** |
| 16 – 25 | **Extreme** |

✅ **Result:** $R_{score} = 20$ falls in the **EXTREME** category. Immediate mitigation mandatory.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Residual Risk After Capacity Augmentation

A district's base risk is computed as $R_{base} = H \times V \times E = 8.0$ units.

After implementing community training, early-warning systems, and seismic retrofitting, the capacity factor improves to $C = 2.5$.

**Step 1:** Apply residual risk formula:

$$R_{residual} = \frac{H \times V \times E}{C}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$R_{residual} = \frac{8.0}{2.5}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate:

$$R_{residual} = 3.2 \text{ units}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 3.13× improvement in capacity factor ($C$ rose from baseline 1.0 to 2.5) reduced the risk by **60%**. This is the *quantitative justification* for disaster preparedness investments — the entire economic premise of emergency planning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Risk Equation — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Hazard]
    V[Vulnerability]
    E[Exposure]
    C[Capacity]
    R[Residual Risk]
    OUT[Disaster Outcome]

    H --> M1[Multiply]
    V --> M1
    E --> M1
    M1 --> DIV[Divide by]
    C --> DIV
    DIV --> R
    R --> OUT[Loss of life, property, livelihood]

    style H fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style V fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style C fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style R fill:#9B5DE5,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style OUT fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style M1 fill:#EEE,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style DIV fill:#EEE,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Vulnerability Component Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Vulnerability V]
    V --> PV[Physical Vulnerability]
    V --> SV[Social Vulnerability]
    V --> EV[Economic Vulnerability]
    V --> EV2[Environmental Vulnerability]

    PV --> P1[Building fragility]
    PV --> P2[Infrastructure age]
    PV --> P3[Construction quality]

    SV --> S1[Age and gender]
    SV --> S2[Literacy and awareness]
    SV --> S3[Social networks]

    EV --> E1[Livelihood type]
    EV --> E2[Income diversity]
    EV --> E3[Insurance coverage]

    EV2 --> EN1[Ecosystem dependence]
    EV2 --> EN2[Resource access]

    style V fill:#9B5DE5,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style PV fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SV fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style EV fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style EV2 fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

## 4.3 Risk Assessment Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Hazard Identification]
    S2[Step 2: Probability Estimation]
    S3[Step 3: Exposure Mapping]
```
