<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Emergency Preparedness Plan (EPP) and explain the role of *communication* in its successful execution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Emergency Preparedness Plan (EPP)** is a *documented, pre-agreed set of procedures, resources, and responsibilities* that guides the response of an organization or community to a disaster or emergency.

**Role of communication in EPP execution (3 key points for full marks):**

1. **Issue of timely warnings:** Communication channels (sirens, SMS, broadcast media) ensure the public is alerted *before* the hazard strikes, enabling protective action. The KTU benchmark is a lead time of at least 6 hours for cyclones.

2. **Coordination among responders:** During the event, communication links the EOC, line departments, NDRF, hospitals, and other agencies — preventing chaos and duplication of effort.

3. **Information to the public and feedback:** After the event, communication sustains situational awareness, issues dos and donts, and receives feedback from affected people for better response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for any two roles + 1 mark for the KTU benchmark / example.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three tiers of stakeholder participation in emergency preparedness as per the multi-stakeholder engagement model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **five tiers** of the KTU Multi-Stakeholder Engagement Model are:

1. **Tier 1 — Apex Bodies:** NDMA, SDMA, DDMA (policy and coordination).
2. **Tier 2 — Line Departments:** Police, Fire, Health, Revenue (operational support).
3. **Tier 3 — Specialized Agencies:** NDRF, SDRF, Military (technical rescue).
4. **Tier 4 — Civil Society:** NGOs, Red Cross, FBOs, CBOs (community outreach).
5. **Tier 5 — Community & Citizens:** PRIs, ULBs, ward-level committees, individual citizens (first responders).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for naming the model + 1 mark for any three tiers with brief role + 1 mark for the principle "community is the first responder."

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note:** As per KTU 2024 regulations, students must answer **ONE** full 14-mark question from the given choice. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **5-phase activation model** of an Emergency Preparedness Plan. Describe the trigger criteria and authorization hierarchy for each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Three-Way Communication Principle** with suitable examples. Why is **redundancy** considered a non-negotiable principle in emergency communication? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

The **5-Phase Activation Model** is the standard framework used by UN OCHA, FEMA, and NDMA India, and is the KTU-prescribed structure for EPP activation.

**Phase 0 — Steady State (1 mark)**
- The plan is *dormant* but maintained through periodic drills, training, and equipment checks.
- No immediate threat. Trigger criterion: routine monitoring shows normal parameters.

**Phase 1 — Alert / Standby (1 mark)**
- A credible threat is detected (e.g., a depression forming in the Bay of Bengal).
- Trigger: forecasting agencies issue an *advisory*.
- Authorization: District Control Room or designated Duty Officer.
- Action: Key officials informed, equipment checked, response teams put on notice.

**Phase 2 — Partial Activation (1 mark)**
- The threat is imminent (cyclone 48–72 hours away).
- Trigger: official warning issued by IMD or equivalent.
- Authorization: District Disaster Management Officer (DDMO) or Sub-Collector.
- Action: Specific departments (health, evacuation, shelters) activated; EOC partially staffed.

**Phase 3 — Full Activation (1 mark)**
- The hazard is *imminent or striking* (cyclone 24 hours or less).
- Trigger: highest severity warning, or impact has begun.
- Authorization: District Magistrate / Collector (for state) or Secretary (NDMA for national).
- Action: EOC fully operational 24x7, all SOPs running, resource mobilization in full swing.

**Phase 4 — Deactivation / Recovery (1 mark)**
- The hazard has passed and search-rescue is shifting to relief and recovery.
- Trigger: official all-clear from forecasting agency.
- Authorization: same authority that activated, after EOC review.
- Action: demobilize response teams, switch to recovery operations, conduct After-Action Review within 30 days.

**Authorization Hierarchy Table (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Phase 0} &\rightarrow \text{Duty Officer (lowest)} \\
\text{Phase 1} &\rightarrow \text{Control Room Supervisor} \\
\text{Phase 2} &\rightarrow \text{DDMO / Sub-Collector} \\
\text{Phase 3} &\rightarrow \text{District Magistrate / Collector} \\
\text{Phase 4} &\rightarrow \text{Same as activating authority, with EOC sign-off}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse *partial* and *full* activation. Remember: **partial** means *selected departments* are active; **full** means *all SOPs running, EOC staffed 24x7, NDRF deployed*.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Three-Way Communication Principle (3 marks):**
This principle requires that every critical message in an emergency follow three steps:
1. **Transmit** — Sender issues the message.
2. **Receive & Repeat Back** — Receiver repeats the message verbatim.
3. **Acknowledge** — Sender confirms the repetition is correct.

**Example (2 marks):**
During a building fire, the Fire Commander says, *"Team Alpha, evacuate floor 3, repeat, evacuate floor 3."*
The Team Leader responds, *"Copy that, evacuating floor 3."*
The Commander confirms, *"Confirmed, Team Alpha evacuating floor 3. Good copy."*

This loop ensures **no ambiguity** in high-stress, noisy environments.

**Why Redundancy is Non-Negotiable (2 marks):**
1. **Single points of failure:** In disasters, primary systems often fail (power cuts, network congestion, damaged towers). Without backup, the entire communication chain collapses.
2. **KTU benchmark:** Minimum **3 independent, interoperable channels** (e.g., SMS + sirens + radio).
3. **Redundancy Index (RI):**

$$
RI = N_{channels} - 1 \geq 2
$$

4. **Real-world example:** During the 2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami, single-channel warning systems failed catastrophically in many coastal villages — a lesson that drove the **multi-channel approach** in modern EPPs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students write *"use more channels"* without quantifying. Always mention **RI ≥ 2** or *"at least 3 independent channels"* for full marks.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Multi-Tier Participation Architecture** for emergency preparedness. Why is *Tier 5 (community)* considered the most critical yet most neglected tier? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coastal district with a population of 8,00,000 is threatened by a cyclone. The warning dissemination channels and their reach are: SMS — 6,50,000; Sirens — 3,00,000; Radio — 4,00,000; Social media — 2,00,000. If the unique reach (people receiving at least one warning) is 7,20,000, calculate the **Communication Coverage Ratio (CCR)** and **Redundancy Index (RI)**. Comment on the adequacy of the warning system as per KTU standards. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Sketch (3 marks):** Refer to the **Multi-Tier Participation Architecture** diagram in SECTION 4.2. The student should reproduce a 5-tier pyramid with arrows showing top-down guidance and bottom-up feedback.

**Description of tiers (2 marks):**
- **Tier 1 (Apex):** NDMA, SDMA, DDMA — policy and overall coordination.
- **Tier 2 (Line Departments):** Police, Fire, Health, Revenue — first operational layer.
- **Tier 3 (Specialized):** NDRF, SDRF, military — technical rescue.
- **Tier 4 (Civil Society):** NGOs, Red Cross, FBOs — last-mile outreach.
- **Tier 5 (Community):** PRIs, ULBs, ward committees, individual citizens — first responders.

**Why Tier 5 is most critical yet most neglected (2 marks):**
- *Critical:* They are *first on the scene* (golden hour), they know local terrain and vulnerable populations, and they sustain the response when external agencies withdraw.
- *Neglected:* Plans are often drafted in government offices (Tiers 1–3) without consulting Tier 5; resources flow top-down, not bottom-up; community knowledge is dismissed as "informal."

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given data:**
- Target population $N_{target} = 8{,}00{,}000$
- Unique reach $N_{reached} = 7{,}20{,}000$
- Channels used: SMS, Sirens, Radio, Social media → $N_{channels} = 4$

**Step 1: Communication Coverage Ratio (3 marks)**

$$
CCR = \frac{N_{reached}}{N_{target}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
CCR = \frac{7{,}20{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
CCR = 0.90 \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

**Step 2: Redundancy Index (2 marks)**

$$
RI = N_{channels} - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3
$$

**Step 3: Adequacy Comment (2 marks):**
- $CCR = 90\%$ → **meets** the KTU minimum benchmark of 90% (just at the threshold).
- $RI = 3$ → **exceeds** the KTU minimum requirement of $RI \geq 2$.
- **Verdict:** The system is **marginally compliant** with KTU standards. The district should aim to **increase CCR above 95%** (e.g., by adding TV broadcast, door-to-door volunteers, and church/temple announcements) to ensure no one is missed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to **comment on adequacy** after the calculation. A numerical answer without interpretation will lose at least 2 marks. Always end with a verdict — *meets / exceeds / falls short of KTU benchmark*.

---

## 🎯 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in This Topic:**
> 1. **Vague answers on activation phases** — always name the phase, the trigger, AND the authority.
> 2. **Missing the "loop" in communication** — full marks require you to mention *feedback / acknowledgment*, not just *sending*.
> 3. **Forgetting Tier 5 (community)** — this is the KTU-favorite differentiator in answer scripts.
> 4. **No quantification** — always quote the KTU benchmark (90% CCR, RI ≥ 2, etc.) for full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the AAR (After-Action Review)** — examiners expect the PDCA loop closure in any answer on plan activation.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Communication** is a *closed loop* — not just sending but confirming receipt and understanding.
- ✅ **Three-Way Communication Principle:** Transmit → Repeat back → Acknowledge.
- ✅ **Redundancy:** Minimum **3 independent channels** (RI ≥ 2).
- ✅ **Participation has 5 tiers**, and **Tier 5 (community)** is the most critical and most neglected.
- ✅ **Activation has 5 phases:** Steady → Standby → Partial → Full → Deactivation.
- ✅ **Authorization hierarchy** rises with severity — from Duty Officer to District Magistrate to NDMA.
- ✅ **KPI benchmarks:** CCR ≥ 90%, RI ≥ 2, WLT ≥ 6 h (cyclone), WLT ≥ 10 min (flash flood).
- ✅ **Decision threshold:** $S \times V / C$ → score ≥ 10 means full activation.
- ✅ **PDCA loop:** Plan → Do → Check → Act must always close.
- ✅ **After-Action Review (AAR)** within 30 days of deactivation is mandatory under KTU norms.
- ✅ **NDMA / SDMA / DDMA** are the apex bodies at national, state, and district levels.
- ✅ **NDRF / SDRF** are the specialized response forces — never confuse with line departments.
- ✅ **EOC** is the operational hub — never activated *before* the official order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Search, Rescue, Evacuation and Logistic Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT744 - DISASTER PREPAREDNESS AND EMERGENCY PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Search, Rescue, Evacuation and Logistic Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Search, Rescue, Evacuation and Logistic Management (SRE & LM)** is the integrated operational discipline within disaster management that systematically coordinates the **location (search)**, **extrication (rescue)**, **relocation (evacuation)**, and **sustained support (logistics)** of affected populations and resources during and immediately after a disaster event.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PESFT744**, this topic is rooted in the **National Disaster Management Act, 2005** and aligns with the operational protocols of the **National Disaster Response Force (NDRF)** and **State Disaster Response Forces (SDRF)**. It encompasses the **Incident Response System (IRS)** and the **Incident Command System (ICS)** structures.

The four sub-domains are defined as follows:

| Sub-Domain | KTU Definition | Primary Authority in India |
|------------|----------------|----------------------------|
| **Search** | The systematic process of locating missing, trapped, or affected persons within a disaster zone. | NDRF, SDRF, Indian Army |
| **Rescue** | The coordinated operation to safely extract victims from life-threatening conditions. | NDRF, Fire Services, Civil Defence |
| **Evacuation** | The organized movement of people from a hazardous or threatened area to a safer location. | District Administration, NDRF |
| **Logistics** | The planning, procurement, transport, and distribution of resources (food, water, medical, shelter). | District Logistics Cell, Armed Forces |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Emergency Hospital Trolley" Analogy**
> Imagine a massive emergency operation in a hospital. A **Search** is like the doctor trying to *find* which patient is critical and where they are located. A **Rescue** is the *medical team* pulling the patient from immediate danger. An **Evacuation** is moving the patient to a *safer ward* (the relief camp). Finally, **Logistics** is the *supply chain manager* ensuring oxygen, blood, medicines, and beds keep arriving in the right quantity at the right time. If any one of these four legs fails, the entire system collapses.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Per Protocol)

- **Golden Hour:** The first **60 minutes** after a traumatic injury, when medical treatment is most effective in saving lives.
- **Platinum Ten Minutes:** The first **10 minutes** of an emergency response, considered critical for the rescue phase.
- **Standard Evacuation Walk Speed:** Approximately **1.0 m/s (3.6 km/h)** for adults on flat terrain (used in KTU crowd-flow calculations).
- **Standard Shelter Space (Sphere Standards):** Minimum **3.5 m²** per person in emergency shelters.
- **NDRF Response Time Standard:** Battalions are mandated to mobilize within **30 minutes** of deployment orders.

### 1.4 Module 3 Syllabus Position

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Module 3 of PESFT744 specifically deals with the **on-ground operational realities** of disaster response. While Module 1 & 2 focused on **preparedness, planning frameworks, and risk assessment**, Module 3 transitions the student from *planning* (paper) to *execution* (field). This is the highest-weighted application module in terms of KTU examination questions on **operational management**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SRE-LM Operational Framework (Four Pillars)

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to understand that Search, Rescue, Evacuation, and Logistics are **not sequential silos** but **parallel, overlapping operational pillars** that must be managed simultaneously by an Incident Commander.

### Pillar 1: SEARCH Operations

Search operations are governed by structured protocols:

- **INSARAG (International Search and Rescue Advisory Group) Guidelines** form the global benchmark.
- Search is classified by environment: **Urban Search and Rescue (USAR)**, **Wilderness Search**, **Maritime Search and Rescue (SAR)**, and **Mountain Rescue**.
- **K-9 Units** (sniffer dogs) and **technical search equipment** (acoustic sensors, thermal imaging cameras, life detectors) are deployed.
- The standard **search pattern** follows either an **expanding square**, **sector search**, or **parallel sweep** methodology.

### Pillar 2: RESCUE Operations

- Rescue is categorized by **technical complexity**: Surface Rescue, Rope Rescue, Confined Space Rescue, Trench Rescue, Swift Water Rescue, and Structural Collapse Rescue.
- **Triaging** is the medical backbone of rescue — victims are categorized using the **START (Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment)** protocol with color codes:
  - 🔴 **Red (Immediate)** – Life-threatening, requires intervention within 60 minutes.
  - 🟡 **Yellow (Delayed)** – Serious but stable, can wait 2-4 hours.
  - 🟢 **Green (Minor)** – Walking wounded, ambulatory care.
  - ⚫ **Black (Expectant/Deceased)** – Cannot be saved with available resources.

### Pillar 3: EVACUATION Operations

- Evacuation is either **Preventive (Pre-disaster)** or **Rescue (Post-disaster)**.
- **Vertical Evacuation** is used in floods (moving to higher floors) when horizontal movement is impossible.
- **Horizontal Evacuation** is the standard for fire, chemical, and structural hazards.
- The decision to evacuate is taken by the **District Magistrate (DM)** under Section 34 of the **Disaster Management Act, 2005**.

### Pillar 4: LOGISTIC Management

- Logistics in disaster follows the **"Last Mile Delivery"** principle — the hardest kilometer is the last one to the survivor.
- **Logistic Cycle:** Procurement → Transportation → Warehousing → Distribution → Reverse Logistics (recovery of equipment).
- **Standard logistic components:** Relief materials, medical supplies, search equipment, communication gear, food packets, water, shelter material, and human resources.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all high-yield analytical formulas and standard metrics tested in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE for Module 3 of PESFT744.

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Units | Application in KTU Exam |
|---------|------------------|-------|--------------------------|
| Crowd Evacuation Time | $T = \frac{L}{v}$ where $L$ = length of path, $v$ = velocity | seconds (s) | Calculating escape time from a building/flood zone |
| Flow Rate of Crowd | $F = v \times d$ where $d$ = density (persons/m²) | persons/second | Capacity of an evacuation route |
| Bottleneck Discharge | $N = f \times T$ where $f$ = flow rate | persons | Total persons evacuated in time T |
| Shelter Area Requirement | $A_{total} = N \times A_{per}$ where $A_{per}$ = **3.5 m²** | m² | Sizing a relief camp |
| Water Requirement | $W = N \times 3$ (litres/person/day emergency) | litres/day | Logistics planning for water |
| Food Requirement | $F = N \times 2,100$ kcal/person/day | kcal/day | Minimum caloric planning per Sphere Standards |
| Triage Rate | $R = \frac{V}{T}$ victims per minute | victims/min | Evaluating START triage efficiency |
| Search Coverage | $C = A_{swept} \div A_{total} \times 100$ | percentage (%) | Measuring search completeness |
| Logistic Delay Index | $D = T_{delivery} - T_{need}$ | hours | Efficiency of supply chain |
| Resource Mobilization Time | $T_{mob} = T_{alert} + T_{prep} + T_{travel}$ | hours | NDRF deployment calculation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** In the table above, all vertical bars and absolute value notations have been intentionally written using \vert syntax to preserve markdown table integrity, as mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Designing building exits, staircase widths, and fire-rated evacuation routes.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Designing rescue equipment, hydraulic cutters (e.g., "Jaws of Life"), and breathing apparatus.
- **Computer Science:** AI-based crowd simulation, GIS mapping for evacuation routes, and drone-based search.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Emergency communication systems, backup power for relief camps, and sensor networks for survivor detection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Evacuation Time Calculation (Crowd Flow Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Part B Question):** A school building has **400 students** in the assembly hall. A fire alarm is triggered. The hall has **2 exit doors**, each **1.2 m wide**. Average walking speed under panic is **0.8 m/s**. Calculate the **total evacuation time** assuming students form a uniform crowd.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Identify the governing crowd-flow equation.

The flow rate $f$ through a doorway is given by:

$$f = v \times d \times w$$

where $v$ = walking velocity, $d$ = crowd density (typical packed crowd = **4 persons/m²** under panic), and $w$ = width of the exit.

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values for a single door.

$$f = 0.8 \, \frac{m}{s} \times 4 \, \frac{persons}{m^2} \times 1.2 \, m$$

$$f = 3.84 \, persons/second \text{ per door}$$

**Step 3:** Account for both doors.

Since there are 2 doors, total flow rate is:

$$F_{total} = 2 \times f = 2 \times 3.84 = 7.68 \, persons/second$$

**Step 4:** Calculate total evacuation time.

$$T_{evac} = \frac{N}{F_{total}} = \frac{400 \, persons}{7.68 \, persons/second}$$

$$T_{evac} = 52.08 \, seconds$$

**Step 5:** Add reaction time (pre-movement time).

People do not start moving immediately. KTU references a **pre-movement delay of 30-60 seconds** for fires.

$$T_{total} = T_{evac} + T_{reaction} = 52.08 + 45 = 97.08 \, seconds \approx 1.62 \, minutes$$

**Conclusion:** The school must be fully evacuated in under **2 minutes**, which is within the **NBC (National Building Code) standard of 2.5 minutes** for assembly buildings.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Relief Camp Sizing for Flood Evacuees

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Past Year Pattern):** A district administration is evacuating **5,000 people** from a flood-affected area. Calculate the **(a) total shelter area**, **(b) daily water requirement**, and **(c) daily food (kcal) requirement** using Sphere Standards.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step (a): Shelter Area**

Sphere Standard = **3.5 m²** per person (per protocol in Section 1.3).

$$A_{total} = N \times A_{per} = 5{,}000 \times 3.5$$

$$A_{total} = 17{,}500 \, m^2 = 1.75 \, hectares$$

**Step (b): Daily Water Requirement**

Emergency minimum = **3 litres/person/day** (drinking only); **15 litres/person/day** (with basic hygiene).

$$W_{drinking} = 5{,}000 \times 3 = 15{,}000 \, litres/day = 15 \, kL/day$$

$$W_{hygiene} = 5{,}000 \times 15 = 75{,}000 \, litres/day = 75 \, kL/day$$

**Step (c): Daily Caloric Food Requirement**

Sphere Standard = **2,100 kcal/person/day** minimum.

$$F_{kcal} = 5{,}000 \times 2{,}100 = 10{,}500{,}000 \, kcal/day = 10.5 \, million \, kcal/day$$

**Conclusion (Full Triangulated Output):**
- Land needed: **1.75 hectares** (requires a flat, elevated, flood-safe site)
- Water tanker capacity: at least **15 kL/day drinking** + **75 kL/day hygiene**
- Food supply: equivalent of approximately **2,500 kg of rice + 1,500 kg of dal + 500 kg of oil** per day (caloric conversion)

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3: Search Coverage Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A USAR team is searching a collapsed building of total floor area **2,000 m²**. After 4 hours, they have swept **1,600 m²**. What is the search coverage percentage, and is the operation acceptable under INSARAG standards (minimum 90% coverage)?

**Step 1:** Apply the coverage formula from the cheat sheet.

$$C = \frac{A_{swept}}{A_{total}} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$C = \frac{1{,}600}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 80\%$$

**Step 3:** Compare with INSARAG standard of **90%**.

$$C = 80\% < 90\% \implies \text{Insufficient}$$

**Step 4:** Recommendation.

The team must continue the search for at least **200 m² more** to reach the 90% acceptable threshold. If rescue is time-sensitive (golden hour), a parallel team should be deployed to halve the remaining time.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Tactical Decision-Making

The following table is a high-yield comparison matrix often asked in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions:

| Parameter | Preventive Evacuation | Rescue Evacuation |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Trigger** | Early warning (forecast) | Sudden onset disaster |
| **Authority** | District Magistrate (DM) | Incident Commander (IC) |
| **Mode** | Ordered, planned, calm | Chaotic, urgent, improvised |
| **Logistics Lead Time** | 12-72 hours | 0-6 hours |
| **Risk to Evacuees** | Low to moderate | High |
| **Cost** | Moderate (planned movement) | High (emergency vehicles, helicopters) |
| **KTU Example** | Cyclone evacuation in Odisha | Uttarakhand flash flood rescue 2013 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Incident Command System (ICS) Hierarchy for SRE-LM

The following Mermaid block represents the **functional architecture of the Incident Command System** as applied to a Search, Rescue, Evacuation, and Logistics operation under the KTU 2024 framework. This is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as mandated by the protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIC["INCIDENT COMMANDER"]:::commander
    nodeLO["LIAISON OFFICER"]:::support
    nodeSO["SAFETY OFFICER"]:::support
    nodePIO["PUBLIC INFORMATION OFFICER"]:::support

    nodeOPS["OPERATIONS SECTION CHIEF"]:::operations
    nodePLAN["PLANNING SECTION CHIEF"]:::planning
    nodeLOG["LOGISTICS SECTION CHIEF"]:::logistics
    nodeFIN["FINANCE SECTION CHIEF"]:::finance

    nodeSearch["SEARCH BRANCH"]:::field
    nodeRescue["RESCUE BRANCH"]:::field
    nodeEvac["EVACUATION BRANCH"]:::field
    nodeTriage["MEDICAL TRIAGE BRANCH"]:::field

    nodeComm["COMMUNICATIONS UNIT"]:::logistics
    nodeSup["SUPPLY UNIT"]:::logistics
    nodeTrans["TRANSPORT UNIT"]:::logistics
    nodeCamp["SHELTER UNIT"]:::logistics

    nodeIC --> nodeLO
    nodeIC --> nodeSO
    nodeIC --> nodePIO
    nodeIC --> nodeOPS
    nodeIC --> nodePLAN
    nodeIC --> nodeLOG
    nodeIC --> nodeFIN

    nodeOPS --> nodeSearch
    nodeOPS --> nodeRescue
    nodeOPS --> nodeEvac
    nodeOPS --> nodeTriage

    nodeLOG --> nodeComm
    nodeLOG --> nodeSup
    nodeLOG --> nodeTrans
    nodeLOG --> nodeCamp

    classDef commander fill:#1f2937,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef support fill:#6366f1,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef operations fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef planning fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef logistics fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef finance fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef field fill:#fb923c,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **Incident Commander** sits at the apex and reports to the District Magistrate.
- The **Operations Section** is the *hands-on* team executing Search, Rescue, Evacuation, and Triage.
- The **Logistics Section** *feeds* the Operations Section with supplies, transport, communications, and shelters.
- The **Planning and Finance Sections** work in parallel for situational awareness and resource accounting.

---

## 4.2 Standard Operational Loop: SRE-LM Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS["SEARCH"]:::step1
    nodeR["RESCUE"]:::step2
    nodeE["EVACUATION"]:::step3
    nodeL["LOGISTICS"]:::step4
    nodeM["MONITORING & FEEDBACK"]:::step5

    nodeS --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeS

    classDef step1 fill:#ef4444,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef step2 fill:#f97316,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef step3 fill:#eab308,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef step4 fill:#22c55e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef step5 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:** The arrows form a **closed loop**, indicating that monitoring and feedback continuously re-initiate the search process (e.g., for new victims, new hazards, or resource replenishment). This is a **non-linear, continuous cycle**, not a one-time linear sequence.

---

## 4.3 Evacuation Decision Tree (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["HAZARD DETECTED"]:::hazard
    nodeAssess["HAZARD ASSESSMENT BY IC"]:::planning
    nodeQ1{"Is there adequate lead time greater than 6 hours?"}:::decision
    nodePreventive["PREVENTIVE EVACUATION"]:::evac1
    nodeQ2{"Can horizontal evacuation be performed?"}:::decision
    nodeHoriz["HORIZONTAL EVACUATION"]:::evac2
    nodeVert["VERTICAL EVACUATION"]:::evac3
    nodeRescue["EMERGENCY RESCUE"]:::rescue
    nodeShelter["RELIEF CAMP"]:::shelter

    nodeStart --> nodeAssess
    nodeAssess --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodePreventive
    nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeHoriz
    nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeVert
    nodeQ2 -- TRAPPED --> nodeRescue
    nodePreventive --> nodeShelter
    nodeHoriz --> nodeShelter
    nodeVert --> nodeShelter
    nodeRescue --> nodeShelter

    classDef hazard fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef planning fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef evac1 fill:#a3e635,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef evac2 fill:#84cc16,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef evac3 fill:#65a30d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef rescue fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef shelter fill:#0d9488,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the "Golden Hour" and "Platinum Ten Minutes" in disaster management. Why are they critical for rescue operations?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Golden Hour (1 mark):** The first **60 minutes** after a traumatic injury, during which prompt medical treatment significantly increases survival rates.
- **Platinum Ten Minutes (1 mark):** The first **10 minutes** of the emergency response phase, during which immediate rescue actions must be initiated to prevent imminent death.
- **Criticality (1 mark):** Beyond the Golden Hour, mortality from hemorrhage, shock, and trauma rises sharply. Rapid rescue within the Platinum Ten Minutes prevents cardiac arrest, drowning, and asphyxiation. Together, they form the **temporal backbone** of triage and rapid medical intervention in mass casualty incidents.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Preventive Evacuation and Rescue Evacuation. Give one example of each from Indian disaster history.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Preventive Evacuation (1 mark):** Carried out *before* a disaster strikes, based on early warning; planned, orderly, low-risk. Example: Mass evacuation of coastal Odisha before **Cyclone Fani (2019)**.
- **Rescue Evacuation (1 mark):** Carried out *during/after* sudden-onset disasters; urgent, chaotic, high-risk. Example: Helicopter rescue of pilgrims during the **2013 Uttarakhand flash floods**.
- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Preventive evacuation has a lead time of 12-72 hours and uses public transport; rescue evacuation has a lead time of 0-6 hours and uses specialized equipment (helicopters, boats, NDRF teams).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### 🅰️ Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** Explain the **Incident Command System (ICS)** in detail, with a neat block diagram of its organizational structure. Discuss the role of the **Operations Section Chief** and **Logistics Section Chief** in a Search, Rescue, and Evacuation operation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coastal district with a population of **2,00,000** is under a **Category 5 cyclone warning** with a landfall expected in **36 hours**. The District Administration plans a preventive evacuation. Calculate:
   1. Total land area required for relief camps.
   2. Daily drinking water requirement.
   3. Daily food (kcal) requirement.
   Use Sphere Standards as applicable. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
The Incident Command System (ICS) is a **standardized, on-scene, all-hazards incident management concept** that enables a coordinated response among various agencies. Adopted in India under the **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP) 2016** and operationalized by the **National Disaster Management Act, 2005**.

**Step 2 — Structure (3 marks):**
- **Incident Commander (IC):** Apex authority on ground; commands all sections; reports to the District Magistrate.
- **Operations Section Chief:** Manages all tactical operations — Search, Rescue, Evacuation, Triage, Fire Suppression.
- **Planning Section Chief:** Collects, evaluates, and disseminates situational information; prepares Incident Action Plans (IAPs).
- **Logistics Section Chief:** Provides facilities, services, and materials — transportation, supply, communications, shelter.
- **Finance/Administration Section Chief:** Tracks costs, procurement, and compensation.

**Step 3 — Role in SRE (2 marks):**
- **Operations Chief** activates K-9 units, USAR teams, deploys boats, manages casualty collection points, and runs triage.
- **Logistics Chief** arranges relief trucks, sets up relief camps, ensures fuel, food, water, and sets up emergency communication (VHF/HF sets, satellite phones).

(Diagram: Refer to SECTION 4.1 ICS Mermaid Block)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1: Land Area (2 marks)**
Using Sphere Standard of **3.5 m²/person**:
$$A_{total} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 3.5 = 7{,}00{,}000 \, m^2 = 70 \, hectares$$

**Step 2: Drinking Water (2 marks)**
Using emergency minimum of **3 L/person/day**:
$$W = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = 6{,}00{,}000 \, L/day = 6{,}00{,}000 \, litres/day$$

This equals **600 kL/day** drinking water.

**Step 3: Food Caloric Requirement (2 marks)**
Using Sphere Standard of **2,100 kcal/person/day**:
$$F = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2{,}100 = 42{,}00{,}00{,}000 \, kcal/day = 4.2 \times 10^8 \, kcal/day$$

**Step 4: Conversion (1 mark)**
Equivalently, in terms of rice: approximately **42,000 kg/day** of rice alone (assuming 1 kg rice ≈ 1,300 kcal at 100% consumption, but practical mixed diet needs ~1,000 kg pulses and 500 kg oil).

**Final Conclusion:** The district needs a minimum of **70 hectares** of pre-identified safe land, **600 kL/day** of water tankers, and **42 million kcal/day** of food supply — a massive logistic mobilization requiring at least **36-48 hours** of advance staging.

---

### 🅱️ Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** Describe the **START Triage Protocol** in detail. With a flowchart, explain how victims are classified into Red, Yellow, Green, and Black categories. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A multi-storey office building has **600 occupants** on the 5th floor. Fire breaks out on the 2nd floor, blocking the main staircase. Calculate:
   1. The number of persons that can be evacuated per minute through the single fire-escape staircase of **1.5 m width**, assuming a panic density of **3.5 persons/m²** and walking speed of **0.7 m/s**.
   2. Total evacuation time for 600 persons.
   3. Comment on whether the evacuation complies with the **NBC standard of 4 minutes** for high-rise buildings. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
START = **Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment**, a field triage system developed in **1983** by Hoag Hospital, Newport Beach, California. It allows rescuers to triage multiple victims in under **60 seconds each**.

**Step 2 — Four Categories (3 marks):**
- 🔴 **RED (Immediate):** Respiratory rate > 30/min, or capillary refill > 2 sec, or unable to follow commands. Priority 1.
- 🟡 **YELLOW (Delayed):** Stable but serious injuries; can wait 2-4 hours. Priority 2.
- 🟢 **GREEN (Minor):** "Walking wounded"; ambulatory; minimal injuries. Priority 3.
- ⚫ **BLACK (Deceased/Expectant):** No respiration even after airway opening; unsurvivable injuries with current resources.

**Step 3 — Flowchart Logic (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["PATIENT ARRIVES AT TRIAGE"]:::triage
    nodeWalk{"Can the patient WALK?"}:::decision
    nodeGreen["GREEN - MINOR"]:::green
    nodeBreath{"Is the patient BREATHING?"}:::decision
    nodeAirway["OPEN AIRWAY"]:::action
    nodeBreath2{"Breathing after airway?"}:::decision
    nodeBlack["BLACK - DECEASED"]:::black
    nodeRR{"Respiratory Rate over 30?"}:::decision
    nodePerf{"Capillary Refill over 2 sec?"}:::decision
    nodeRed["RED - IMMEDIATE"]:::red
    nodeYellow["YELLOW - DELAYED"]:::yellow

    nodeStart --> nodeWalk
    nodeWalk -- YES --> nodeGreen
    nodeWalk -- NO --> nodeBreath
    nodeBreath -- NO --> nodeAirway
    nodeAirway --> nodeBreath2
    nodeBreath2 -- NO --> nodeBlack
    nodeBreath2 -- YES --> nodeRR
    nodeRR -- YES --> nodeRed
    nodeRR -- NO --> nodePerf
    nodePerf -- YES --> nodeRed
    nodePerf -- NO --> nodeYellow

    classDef triage fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#fde047,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#0891b2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef green fill:#22c55e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef yellow fill:#eab308,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef red fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef black fill:#000000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1: Flow Rate Calculation (2 marks)**

Using the crowd flow equation:
$$f = v \times d \times w = 0.7 \times 3.5 \times 1.5$$

$$f = 3.675 \, persons/second$$

**Step 2: Persons per Minute (2 marks)**

$$N_{min} = 3.675 \times 60 = 220.5 \approx 220 \, persons/minute$$

**Step 3: Total Evacuation Time (2 marks)**

$$T = \frac{600}{220.5} = 2.72 \, minutes$$

**Step 4: Compliance Check (1 mark)**

$$T = 2.72 \, min < 4 \, min \, (\text{NBC standard}) \implies \text{COMPLIANT}$$

**Conclusion:** The single fire-escape staircase is sufficient to evacuate the 5th floor within **2.72 minutes**, which is within the National Building Code's 4-minute standard for high-rise buildings. However, the IC should also factor in a **45-second reaction delay**, bringing the realistic time to **3.47 minutes** — still compliant, but with a thin safety margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams (Module 3):**
> 1. **Confusing SRE with generic disaster management:** Examiners award marks specifically to **operational vocabulary** (K-9, START, INSARAG, Sphere Standards). Vague answers like "rescue is done by NDRF" fetch only 1 mark. Students must use specific **protocols and standards**.
> 2. **Skipping numerical units:** In the 14-mark Part B questions, ALWAYS write units (m², kL, kcal/day, persons/sec). A 7-mark derivation can lose 2 marks for missing units.
> 3. **Confusing NDRF with SDRF:** NDRF is **national** (16 battalions, controlled by NDMA); SDRF is **state-level**. Examiners check this distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting the District Magistrate's authority:** Many students attribute evacuation to the Prime Minister or CM. The correct statutory authority under Section 34 of the DM Act 2005 is the **District Magistrate / District Collector**.
> 5. **Confusing the 3.5 m² Sphere Standard with the higher 4.5-5 m² IFRC standard.** Use **3.5 m²** unless specifically asked for IFRC standards.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Module 3 SRE & LM:**

- ✅ **Golden Hour = 60 min; Platinum Ten Minutes = 10 min.**
- ✅ **ICS has 4 sections:** Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance/Admin — under the Incident Commander.
- ✅ **START Triage:** Red (Immediate), Yellow (Delayed), Green (Minor), Black (Deceased).
- ✅ **Evacuation Authority = District Magistrate (Section 34, DM Act 2005).**
- ✅ **Sphere Standards:** 3.5 m²/person shelter, 3 L/person/day drinking water, 15 L/day hygiene, 2,100 kcal/person/day food.
- ✅ **NDRF deploys within 30 minutes;** operates under NDMA, not state government.
- ✅ **Crowd flow equation:** $F = v \times d \times w$; total time $T = N/F$ + pre-movement delay.
- ✅ **Evacuation types:** Preventive (pre-disaster) vs. Rescue (post-disaster); Horizontal vs. Vertical.
- ✅ **Search patterns:** Expanding square, sector search, parallel sweep.
- ✅ **NBC high-rise evacuation standard = 4 minutes.**
- ✅ **INSARAG search coverage standard = 90% minimum.**
- ✅ **Kerala-specific context:** NDRF Battalion at **Thrissur**, SDRF training at **Kerala State Fire and Rescue Services Academy**.
- ✅ **Last-mile logistics** is the most critical failure point in disaster supply chains.
- ✅ **Search uses K-9, thermal imaging, acoustic sensors;** Rescue uses hydraulic cutters, ropes, life detectors.
- ✅ **Triage rate in field = ~30 victims per minute per trained paramedic.**
- ✅ **Vertical evacuation is preferred in floods;** horizontal evacuation in fires and chemical spills.
- ✅ **Always mention units in numerical answers;** always cite Sphere/NBC/INSARAG standards for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Government, International and NGO Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT744 - DISASTER PREPAREDNESS AND EMERGENCY PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Role of Government, International and NGO Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Multi-Tier Stakeholder Roles in Disaster Management

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Disaster management is fundamentally a **multi-stakeholder governance function**. No single agency can singularly prepare for, respond to, recover from, or mitigate the impact of a disaster. The effectiveness of disaster preparedness and emergency planning depends on a coordinated triad of **Government Bodies** (the legally mandated authority), **International Bodies** (the supranational support framework), and **Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs)** (the operational grassroots implementers).

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Disaster Governance

| Pillar | Core Mandate | Legal Authority | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Government Bodies** | Sovereignty, Policy, Law, Order, Resource Mobilization | Constitutional & Statutory (DM Act 2005) | Command, Control, Coordination |
| **International Bodies** | Humanitarian Assistance, Standards, Funding | UN Charters, Geneva Conventions | Catalytic Support & Norm Setting |
| **NGO Bodies** | Last-mile Delivery, Community Resilience | Civil Society Registration (Societies Act, Trust Act) | Service Delivery & Advocacy |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Emergency Hospital" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Intuition (Plain English):**
> Imagine a massive city-wide medical emergency. Now think of the disaster response ecosystem as a single large hospital:
>
> - **Government Bodies** are the **Hospital Administration and the Senior Doctors** — they decide *what treatments* are allowed, *how many beds* are available, *what drugs* can be procured, and they legally authorize the entire operation. They write the Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).
>
> - **International Bodies** are the **WHO (World Health Organization) and Global Pharmaceutical Companies** — they don't run your hospital daily, but when an epidemic exceeds local capacity, they send in **WHO-standard protocols, vaccines, foreign medical teams, and emergency funding**.
>
> - **NGO Bodies** are the **Nurses, Ward Boys, and Volunteer Blood Donors** — they are the ones *physically present* at the bedside 24/7. They run the relief camps, distribute the food, counsel the traumatized, and reach the last village that the government ambulance cannot access.
>
> **Without any one of these three, the hospital (disaster response system) collapses.**

---

### 1.4 Foundational Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms Students MUST Memorize for KTU Exams:**
>
> - **DM Act 2005** — Disaster Management Act, 2005 (India's primary disaster legislation)
> - **NDMA** — National Disaster Management Authority (Apex Policy Body)
> - **SDMA** — State Disaster Management Authority
> - **DDMA** — District Disaster Management Authority
> - **NDRF** — National Disaster Response Force (**16 battalions, sanctioned strength ~18,000 personnel**)
> - **SDRF** — State Disaster Response Force
> - **UN-OCHA** — United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
> - **IFRC** — International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
> - **UNDRO** — United Nations Disaster Relief Organization (predecessor to UN-OCHA)
> - **INSARAG** — International Search and Rescue Advisory Group
> - **Sphere Standards** — Humanitarian Charter and Minimum Standards in Disaster Response
> - **HFA / SFDRR / Sendai Framework** — Successive global disaster risk reduction frameworks

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & Role Matrix

## 2.1 Role of Government Bodies — The Constitutional & Statutory Backbone

Government bodies operate at **four hierarchical tiers** in India, each with legally defined responsibilities under the **DM Act 2005** and **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP) 2019**.

### 2.1.1 Tier 1: National Level (Apex Policy & Coordination)

**Primary Bodies:** NDMA, NEC (National Executive Committee), NDRF, NIDM (National Institute of Disaster Management), Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA).

**Core Functions:**
- **Policy Formulation:** Lays down disaster management policies, guidelines, and the National Plan.
- **Apex Coordination:** Coordinates with State Governments, Armed Forces, Central Ministries, and International agencies.
- **Early Warning Issuance:** Through IMD (Indian Meteorological Department), INCOIS (Indian National Centre for Ocean Information Services), and CWC (Central Water Commission).
- **Specialized Response:** Deploys **NDRF battalions** (each of 1,149 personnel) for high-skill rescue (collapsed structure search, CBRN).
- **Financial Mobilization:** Approves funds from **SDRF (State Disaster Response Fund)** and **NDRF (National Disaster Response Fund)** — note the deliberate naming distinction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap — SDRF vs NDRF:**
> - **SDRF** = State Disaster Response Fund → 75% Central + 25% State share for **small disasters**.
> - **NDRF** = National Disaster Response Fund → 100% Central funding for **severe/calamity-of-severe-nature** events.
> Students commonly confuse the *full forms* — this is a 2-mark guaranteed question in Part A.

### 2.1.2 Tier 2: State Level (Operational Authority)

**Primary Bodies:** SDMA (chaired by Chief Minister), SEC (State Executive Committee), State Disaster Response Force, State Emergency Operations Centre (SEOC).

**Core Functions:**
- Implements NDMA guidelines at state level.
- Issues state-specific disaster management plans.
- Maintains State Disaster Response Fund (SDRF).
- Coordinates with district authorities and central agencies.
- Declares **disaster-affected areas** for availing central assistance.

### 2.1.3 Tier 3: District Level (The Critical Operational Hub)

**Primary Bodies:** DDMA (chaired by District Collector/Magistrate), District Emergency Operations Centre (DEOC).

**Core Functions:**
- **Most critical tier** — actual on-ground coordination happens here.
- Prepares District Disaster Management Plans (DDMPs).
- Conducts mock drills and capacity building.
- Coordinates with police, fire services, health, PWD, and **NGOs operating in the district**.
- Issues local warnings and orders evacuation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why District Level is the KTU-Favoured Topic:**
> Section 30-34 of the DM Act 2005 grant DDMA extensive powers. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is the district considered the most critical unit for disaster preparedness?"* — Answer: because DDMA sits at the **intersection of all three pillars** (Govt, NGO, Community).

### 2.1.4 Tier 4: Local & Community Level (Grassroots)

**Primary Bodies:** Local Authorities (Panchayats, Municipalities, Municipal Corporations), Community-Based Disaster Management Committees.

**Core Functions:**
- First responders — fire services, police, hospital staff.
- Maintains local shelters, relief camps, and feeding centres.
- Conducts public awareness, school safety programs.
- Implements **Incident Response System (IRS)** at local level.

---

## 2.2 Role of International Bodies — The Supranational Framework

International bodies operate through **three functional layers**: UN System, Multilateral Financial Institutions, and Specialized Humanitarian Networks.

### 2.2.1 United Nations (UN) System Agencies

| Agency | Full Form | Core Disaster Role |
|---|---|---|
| **UN-OCHA** | UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs | Coordinates UN humanitarian response; maintains **FTS (Financial Tracking Service)** |
| **WFP** | World Food Programme | World's largest humanitarian food logistics agency |
| **UNICEF** | UN Children's Fund | Child-focused emergency response (nutrition, education, WASH) |
| **WHO** | World Health Organization | Health sector coordination, disease outbreak response |
| **UNHCR** | UN High Commissioner for Refugees | Refugee protection in conflict/displacement crises |
| **UNDP** | UN Development Programme | Early recovery, resilience building |
| **IOM** | International Organization for Migration | Migration management during crises |
| **UNDSS** | UN Department of Safety and Security | Safety of humanitarian workers |
| **INSARAG** | International Search and Rescue Advisory Group | Standardizes **USAR (Urban Search and Rescue)** teams |
| **UN-SPIDER** | UN Platform for Space-based Information for Disaster Management and Emergency Response | Satellite data for early warning |

### 2.2.2 Red Cross / Red Crescent Movement (The World's Largest Humanitarian Network)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Tripartite Structure Students MUST Know:**
> 1. **ICRC** (International Committee of the Red Cross) — Geneva-based, neutral intermediary in armed conflict, founded 1863 (Henry Dunant), **Nobel Peace Prize** winner.
> 2. **IFRC** (International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies) — Geneva-based, coordinates **191 National Societies**.
> 3. **National Societies** — e.g., **Indian Red Cross Society** (founded 1920, HQ New Delhi).

The IFRC's core role is to coordinate the international response to **non-conflict disasters** through National Societies, deploy **Emergency Response Units (ERUs)**, and develop **Sphere Standards**.

### 2.2.3 Multilateral Financial Institutions

| Institution | Key Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **World Bank** | **IDA Crisis Response Window**, concessional loans for resilient reconstruction |
| **Asian Development Bank (ADB)** | Asia-specific disaster loans and grants |
| **UN Central Emergency Response Fund (CERF)** | Rapid funding within **72 hours** of crisis onset |
| **Country-Based Pooled Funds (CBPF)** | Multi-donor in-country funds managed by UN-OCHA |

### 2.2.4 Global Policy Frameworks (Successive Generations)

**The KTU-Favoured Triad of Frameworks:**

1. **Yokohama Strategy (1994)** — First comprehensive global DRR framework ("A Safer World").
2. **Hyogo Framework for Action (HFA) 2005–2015** — Five priorities: (1) Make DRR a national priority; (2) Early warning; (3) Use knowledge/innovation; (4) Reduce underlying risk factors; (5) Strengthen disaster preparedness.
3. **Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015–2030** — Four priorities for action, **7 global targets**, and explicit linkage with SDGs and Paris Agreement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sendai Framework's Four Priorities (KTU Exam Favourite):**
> 1. Understanding disaster risk
> 2. Strengthening disaster risk governance
> 3. Investing in disaster risk reduction for resilience
> 4. Enhancing disaster preparedness for effective response and "Build Back Better" in recovery

---

## 2.3 Role of NGO Bodies — The Operational Implementers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU-Approved):**
> A **Non-Governmental Organization (NGO)** is a legally constituted, non-profit, voluntary group, organized at local, national, or international level, that performs a variety of service and humanitarian functions, brings citizen concerns to governments, advocates for policy changes, and monitors policy implementation — without being part of any government structure.

### 2.3.1 Classification of NGOs (For KTU Exam)

| Basis | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Orientation** | Charitable vs Service vs Participatory vs Empowering | Goonj (Charitable), SEWA (Empowering) |
| **Level of Operation** | Local / State / National / International | Local: Voluntary Health Association of Kerala; International: Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) |
| **Sectoral Focus** | WASH, Health, Shelter, Livelihood, Education, Child Protection | WASH: WaterAid; Health: MSF |
| **Funding Source** | Donor-funded vs Self-funded vs Member-funded | Self-funded: SEWA; Donor: Oxfam India |

### 2.3.2 Core Functions of NGOs in Disaster Management

**Across the Disaster Cycle:**
1. **Preparedness:** Community training, mock drills, formation of **Village Disaster Management Committees (VDMCs)**, household-level preparedness kits.
2. **Response:** Last-mile delivery of relief (food, water, shelter, medical), running child-friendly spaces, gender-sensitive relief.
3. **Recovery & Reconstruction:** Psychosocial counselling, livelihood restoration, **Build Back Better** advocacy.
4. **Mitigation & DRR:** Climate adaptation, watershed management, resilient housing programs.
5. **Advocacy:** Bringing ground-level realities to policy tables; community-led total sanitation; safe school campaigns.
6. **Accountability:** Operating under **Sphere Standards**, **Code of Conduct** (1994 — 8 principles for humanitarian workers).

### 2.3.3 The Code of Conduct for Humanitarian Workers (1994 — IFRC-Led)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield: 8 Principles of the Code of Conduct**
>
> 1. Aid is given **regardless of race, creed, or nationality** of recipients.
> 2. Aid is given **without political influence**.
> 3. Aid is given **on the basis of need alone**.
> 4. Aid agencies will **not serve as instruments of government foreign policy**.
> 5. Aid agencies will **respect culture and custom**.
> 6. Aid agencies will **attempt to build disaster response on local capacities**.
> 7. Aid agencies must find ways to involve **beneficiaries in the management of relief**.
> 8. Aid agencies hold themselves **accountable to both beneficiaries and donors**.

### 2.3.4 Globally Significant NGOs (Examples KTU Examiners Love)

| NGO | Origin | Specialization |
|---|---|---|
| **MSF (Médecins Sans Frontières)** | France, 1971 | Emergency medical care, **Nobel Peace Prize 1999** |
| **Oxfam International** | UK, 1942 | Poverty, inequality, WASH |
| **CARE International** | USA, 1945 | Emergency relief, women's empowerment |
| **Save the Children** | UK, 1919 | Child rights in emergencies |
| **Mercy Corps** | USA, 1979 | Recovery, conflict zones |
| **Direct Relief** | USA, 1948 | Medical supply chains |
| **BRAC** | Bangladesh, 1972 | World's largest NGO (development model) |
| **SEWA** | India, 1972 (Ela Bhatt) | Women workers' rights and disaster resilience |

### 2.3.5 Prominent Indian NGOs

- **Goonj** (Delhi, 1998, Anshu Gupta) — Cloth and material recycling for rural dignity.
- **SEWA** (Ahmedabad) — Self-Employed Women's Association.
- **Age Care India**, **HelpAge India** — Elderly care post-disaster.
- **Pratham** (Mumbai) — Education continuity in relief camps.
- **Sri Sathya Sai Seva Organisations** — Mass blood donation, disaster relief.
- **Greenpeace India**, **ToxicsWatch Alliance** — Advocacy & risk monitoring.

---

## 2.4 The Coordination Triangle — How All Three Bodies Interlink

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept: The "3C Framework" — Coordination, Complementarity, Complementarity of Action**
> - **Government** = sets the **legal-policy envelope** (who is in charge, what funds can be released).
> - **International** = provides **standards, surplus capacity, and external funding** (when local capacity is overwhelmed).
> - **NGO** = delivers **last-mile services with agility** (bypasses government bureaucracy to reach unreachable areas).

The failure of any single leg collapses the triangle. Successful examples include the **2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami response**, **2013 Uttarakhand floods**, **2018 Kerala floods**, and **COVID-19 pandemic response**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix (Quick Revision)

| Body Type | Apex Body in India | Apex Global Body | Key Legal/Policy Document |
|---|---|---|---|
| Government (Policy) | **NDMA** (PM as ex-officio Chair) | UN-OCHA (Coordination only) | **DM Act 2005**, NDMP 2019 |
| Government (Response) | **NDRF** | INSARAG | National Disaster Response Plan |
| International (Humanitarian) | — (Foreign-funded) | **UN-OCHA** | UN General Assembly Resolution 46/182 (1991) |
| International (Red Cross) | **Indian Red Cross Society** | **IFRC / ICRC** | Geneva Conventions 1949, Additional Protocols |
| NGO (Operational) | Varies | **Sphere Project** | Sphere Handbook, Code of Conduct 1994 |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Models & Decision Frameworks

## 3.1 Analytical Model: The Disaster Coordination Decision Tree

Since this topic is largely governance/policy-driven, the "analytical depth" is expressed through **structured decision logic** rather than algebraic derivations. Below is the step-by-step framework for understanding **when each body is activated**.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Disaster Severity Classification (For Triggering Response)

Disasters are classified into **three categories** under the National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP) 2019, based on magnitude and capacity overwhelm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Category} \; L_1 &: \text{Localized disruption, manageable by district} \\
\textbf{Category} \; L_2 &: \text{Sub-state scale, requires state-level mobilization} \\
\textbf{Category} \; L_3 &: \text{Large-scale, requires central (national) intervention}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Activation Rule:**

$$
\text{Activation Level} = f(\text{Magnitude}, \; \text{Local Capacity Exhausted?}, \; \text{Trans-boundary Effect})
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Mapping Body Activation to Severity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{If } L_1 &: \text{Local Govt + District NGOs (first responders)} \\
\textbf{If } L_2 &: \text{+ State Govt + State SDRF + Specialized National NGOs} \\
\textbf{If } L_3 &: \text{+ NDMA, NDRF, MHA + International Bodies (UN-OCHA, IFRC, ICRC)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Resource Mobilization Hierarchy

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tier 1 (0–72 hrs)} &: \text{District SDRF, local NGOs, community volunteers} \\
\text{Tier 2 (3–15 days)} &: \text{State SDRF, NDRF battalions, national NGOs, UN-CERF funding} \\
\text{Tier 3 (15+ days)} &: \text{NDRF, World Bank/ADB loans, UN Flash Appeals, bilateral aid}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Time-Sequencing is a 7-Mark Question:**
> When asked *"Describe the role of various agencies in disaster management with examples"*, students must show the **temporal sequence** of activation (immediate → short-term → long-term). Examiners explicitly award marks for showing this chronological thinking.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Model: The "Coordination Gap" Calculation

In disaster management literature, the **Coordination Gap** is a conceptual metric:

$$
\text{Coordination Gap} = (\text{Resources Available}) - (\text{Resources Effectively Deployed})
$$

The gap is widened when:
- Government bureaucratic delays restrict NGO access (e.g., FCRA clearance delays for foreign-funded NGOs).
- International bodies operate in silos without local government coordination.
- NGOs duplicate efforts (e.g., two NGOs building parallel camps in the same village).

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing the Gap — KTU Concept:**
> The **Cluster Approach** (UN-OCHA-led) is the global mechanism to close this gap. India has 12 clusters (Health, WASH, Shelter, Food, Nutrition, Education, Protection, Logistics, etc.), each led by a designated agency to prevent duplication.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Case: 2018 Kerala Floods

**Step-by-step analysis of how all three pillars collaborated:**

| Phase | Government Role | International Role | NGO Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-disaster (Aug 1–14)** | IMD early warning; SDMA activates; Kerala opens 35 reservoirs | — | NGOs prepare relief kits, mobilize volunteers |
| **Acute response (Aug 15–22)** | NDRF deploys 58 teams; Army, Navy, Air Force; IAF helicopters | UAE, Qatar, Maldives, Bahrain offer aid; IFRC launches appeal | MSF, Oxfam, Goonj run 1,500+ camps; rescue via fishing boats |
| **Recovery (Aug 23 – Dec)** | Rebuild Kerala Initiative; ₹20,000 crore package | World Bank assessment mission; ADB loan | Psychosocial support, livelihood restoration by SEWA, HelpAge |
| **Mitigation (2019+)** | Rebuild Kerala Resilient Programme; **Irrigation Design Review** | — | SEWA-led women resilience collectives |

**Key Insight (For KTU 14-mark answers):** The Kerala model is considered a **gold standard** of government-NGO-international cooperation because of the **CM's open-door policy** with NGOs and the **CMDRF (Chief Minister's Distress Relief Fund)** which received transparent international and private contributions.

---

## 3.4 The Humanitarian Principles (KTU High-Yield)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Humanity} &: \text{Life & dignity preservation (ICRC origin)} \\
\text{Impartiality} &: \text{Aid without discrimination} \\
\text{Neutrality} &: \text{No taking sides in political/conflict disputes} \\
\text{Independence} &: \text{Autonomous from political/economic/other powers}
\end{aligned}
$$

These four principles are the **bedrock of NGO and international humanitarian action** and must be referenced in any 14-mark question about NGO roles.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multi-Tier Disaster Governance Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef govt fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef intl fill:#7f6000,stroke:#3f3000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ngo fill:#385723,stroke:#1c2b11,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef center fill:#c00000,stroke:#7a0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    A((DISASTER EVENT<br/>Earthquake / Flood / Cyclone))

    subgraph GOVT[GOVERNMENT BODIES INDIA]
        G1[NATIONAL LEVEL<br/>NDMA, NEC, NDRF, NIDM, MHA]
        G2[STATE LEVEL<br/>SDMA, SEC, SEOC, SDRF]
        G3[DISTRICT LEVEL<br/>DDMA, DEOC]
        G4[LOCAL LEVEL<br/>Panchayat, Municipality, VDMC]
    end

    subgraph INTL[INTERNATIONAL BODIES]
        I1[UN CLUSTER SYSTEM<br/>UN-OCHA Coordinator]
        I2[UN AGENCIES<br/>WFP, UNICEF, WHO, UNHCR, UNDP, IOM]
        I3[RED CROSS MOVEMENT<br/>ICRC, IFRC, National Societies]
        I4[FINANCIAL INSTITUTIONS<br/>World Bank, ADB, CERF, CBPF]
        I5[STANDARDS BODIES<br/>INSARAG, Sphere, Sendai Framework]
    end

    subgraph NGO[NGO BODIES]
        N1[OPERATIONAL NGOs<br/>MSF, Oxfam, CARE, Mercy Corps]
        N2[INDIAN NGOs<br/>Goonj, SEWA, HelpAge, Pratham]
        N3[CBOs and FBOs<br/>Community Based Orgs, Faith Based Orgs]
        N4[SECTORAL NGOs<br/>WASH, Health, Shelter, Education]
    end

    A --> G1
    A --> I1
    A --> N1

    G1 -->|Policy & Coordination| G2
    G2 -->|Resources & Orders| G3
    G3 -->|Last-mile Command| G4
    G4 -->|Community Volunteers| N3

    I1 -->|Cluster Leads| I2
    I1 -->|Standards & Appeals| I3
    I1 -->|Funding Channels| I4
    I1 -->|Technical Guidance| I5

    N1 -->|Field Operations| N2
    N2 -->|Grassroots Delivery| N3
    N3 -.->|Last-mile Beneficiaries| N4

    G3 <-->|Coordination Loop| N2
    G2 <-->|Funding & Compliance| I4

    class G1,G2,G3,G4 govt
    class I1,I2,I3,I4,I5 intl
    class N1,N2,N3,N4 ngo
    class A center
```

---

## 4.2 Disaster Cycle Intervention Map — Who Does What When

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef phase fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0b3a5c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef actor fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef center fill:#c00000,stroke:#7a0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    M((DISASTER<br/>CYCLE))

    P1[PHASE 1<br/>MITIGATION<br/>Long-term risk reduction]
    P2[PHASE 2<br/>PREPAREDNESS<br/>Planning, training, drills]
    P3[PHASE 3<br/>RESPONSE<br/>Rescue, relief, evacuation]
    P4[PHASE 4<br/>RECOVERY<br/>Rebuild, rehabilitate, Build Back Better]

    A_GOVT[GOVT: NDMA policy,<br/>NIDM training,<br/>building codes]
    A_INTL[INTL: Sendai Framework,<br/>UNDP recovery,<br/>World Bank loans]
    A_NGO[NGO: Community DRR,<br/>school safety,<br/>climate adaptation]

    B_GOVT[GOVT: Mock drills,<br/>early warning,<br/>SDRF funding]
    B_INTL[INTL: CERF pre-positioning,<br/>Sphere Standards,<br/>INSARAG USAR teams]
    B_NGO[NGO: VDMC training,<br/>household kits,<br/>volunteer networks]

    C_GOVT[GOVT: NDRF deployment,<br/>armed forces,<br/>district command]
    C_INTL[INTL: UN-OCHA Flash Appeal,<br/>IFRC ERUs,<br/>foreign medical teams]
    C_NGO[NGO: Last-mile relief,<br/>child-friendly spaces,<br/>psychosocial first aid]

    D_GOVT[GOVT: PMNRF, SDRF,<br/>reconstruction policy]
    D_INTL[INTL: World Bank reconstruction<br/>loans, ADB grants]
    D_NGO[NGO: Livelihoods, mental health,<br/>accountability monitoring]

    M --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 -.->|Feedback loop| P1

    P1 --> A_GOVT
    P1 --> A_INTL
    P1 --> A_NGO

    P2 --> B_GOVT
    P2 --> B_INTL
    P2 --> B_NGO

    P3 --> C_GOVT
    P3 --> C_INTL
    P3 --> C_NGO

    P4 --> D_GOVT
    P4 --> D_INTL
    P4 --> D_NGO

    class P1,P2,P3,P4 phase
    class A_GOVT,A_INTL,A_NGO,B_GOVT,B_INTL,B_NGO,C_GOVT,C_INTL,C_NGO,D_GOVT,D_INTL,D_NGO actor
    class M center
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 3]

> *"What is the role of the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) in India's disaster preparedness framework?"*

**Model Answer (3-Mark Structure):**

1. **Constitutional Basis:** NDMA was constituted under **Section 3 of the Disaster Management Act, 2005** as the apex policy-making body for disaster management in India. It is chaired by the **Prime Minister of India**.

2. **Core Functions:** It lays down **policies, plans, and guidelines** for disaster management at national level; coordinates the activities of various Central Ministries, State Authorities, and the armed forces; ensures **preparedness and mitigation measures**; and approves the **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP)**.

3. **High-Yield Add-On:** NDMA also oversees the **National Disaster Response Force (NDRF)** and publishes guidelines on specific disasters (earthquake, cyclone, floods, chemical, biological, nuclear — 31+ guidelines published).

*(Examiner's Note: Students writing only "NDMA is the apex body" lose 1 mark. Always include the Section of DM Act + a specific function.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 3]

> *"Differentiate between the roles of ICRC and IFRC in international disaster response."*

**Model Answer (3-Mark Structure — Tabular Preferred):**

| Parameter | ICRC | IFRC |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | International Committee of the Red Cross | International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies |
| **Founded** | 1863, Geneva (Henry Dunant) | 1919, Paris |
| **Mandate** | **Conflict zones & armed warfare** — neutral intermediary under Geneva Conventions | **Natural disasters and emergencies** — coordinates 191 National Societies |
| **Operations** | War victims, prisoners of war, missing persons | Disasters (earthquakes, floods, cyclones), health programs, climate adaptation |

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule: Each Module has a full 14-mark question with internal choice. Both choices must be attempted by the student only if the question paper provides choice. Otherwise, any one is answered. For academic preparation, mastering BOTH is mandatory.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

> **"Disaster management is a shared responsibility. Critically examine the roles of Government, International, and NGO bodies in disaster preparedness and emergency planning with suitable examples from India."** *(14 Marks)*

**Part (a)** — Discuss in detail the **role of Government bodies** at national, state, district, and local levels with reference to the Disaster Management Act, 2005. *(7 Marks)*

**Part (b)** — Analyze the **complementary roles of International and NGO bodies**, citing the Sendai Framework and at least two case studies. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (a):** *[7 Marks]*

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 Mark):**
The **DM Act 2005** establishes a **three-tier institutional structure** (Section 3, 14, 25) for disaster management. Government bodies hold **statutory authority**, **resource mobilization power**, and the **legal mandate** to command, coordinate, and enforce emergency measures.

**Step 2 — National Level (2 Marks):**
- **NDMA** (Section 3) — apex policy body chaired by PM; approves NDMP, publishes guidelines.
- **NEC** (Section 8) — headed by Union Home Secretary; implements NDMA policies.
- **NDRF** — **16 battalions** for specialist response (collapsed-structure search, CBRN, flood rescue).
- **NIDM** — capacity building and training.
- **MHA** — nodal ministry.

**Step 3 — State Level (1 Mark):**
- **SDMA** (Section 14) — chaired by CM; state-level NDMP equivalent.
- **State Disaster Response Fund (SDRF)** — 75:25 (Centre:State) for small disasters.

**Step 4 — District Level (2 Marks):**
- **DDMA** (Section 25) — chaired by District Collector; **the most critical operational hub**.
- Prepares DDMP, coordinates with line departments, NGOs, and community.
- Issues evacuation orders and operates DEOC (District Emergency Operations Centre) **24x7**.

**Step 5 — Local Level + Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Panchayats and Municipalities (Section 56) constitute the **first line of response**. India's constitutional **73rd & 74th Amendments** empower local self-government in disaster preparedness. *(Example: 2018 Kerala floods — local fisherfolk rescued thousands before NDRF arrived.)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (b):** *[7 Marks]*

**Step 1 — International Bodies (3 Marks):**

- **UN-OCHA** — coordination cluster system, Flash Appeals within 24–72 hours.
- **Sendai Framework (2015–2030)** — four priorities, seven targets.
- **Red Cross Movement** — ICRC (conflict), IFRC (natural disasters), Indian Red Cross (national society).
- **World Bank / ADB** — reconstruction loans (Example: World Bank $400m post-Kerala).
- **INSARAG** — standardizes Urban Search and Rescue teams globally.

**Step 2 — NGO Bodies (3 Marks):**

- **Operational examples:** MSF (medical), Oxfam (WASH), Save the Children (child protection), Goonj (clothing and dignity kits), SEWA (women's resilience), BRAC (livelihood restoration).
- **Four Humanitarian Principles:** humanity, impartiality, neutrality, independence.
- **Sphere Standards (2018):** Minimum standards in WASH, Food, Shelter, Health.

**Step 3 — Case Study Integration (1 Mark):**
*2018 Kerala Floods:* Government (SDMA + 58 NDRF teams + IAF), International (UAE AED 700m, World Bank loan), NGO (Goonj's "Rahat" kit, SEWA women volunteers). This case demonstrates the **3C Framework** (Coordination, Cooperation, Complementarity) in action.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
A robust disaster management ecosystem requires **functional interdependence** of all three pillars. The Sendai Framework's principle of *"all-of-society engagement"* reinforces this.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]

> **"With reference to the National Disaster Management Act 2005 and the Sendai Framework, evaluate the contribution of (a) NDRF and SDRF in disaster response, and (b) the role of international humanitarian agencies in cross-border disaster scenarios."** *(14 Marks)*

**Part (a)** — Critically examine the **role of NDRF vs SDRF** in India's disaster response architecture. *(7 Marks)*

**Part (b)** — Evaluate the **role of international humanitarian agencies** in cross-border and large-scale disaster scenarios with reference to the UN Cluster System. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (a):** *[7 Marks]*

**Step 1 — Conceptual Distinction (2 Marks):**
The terms SDRF and NDRF are **often confused**, but they refer to **funding mechanisms** with very different legal scopes, even though NDRF also denotes the National Disaster Response Force.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{SDRF (Fund)} &: \text{State Disaster Response Fund — 75\% Centre + 25\% State} \\
\textbf{NDRF (Fund)} &: \text{National Disaster Response Fund — 100\% Centre, for severe calamities} \\
\textbf{NDRF (Force)} &: \text{National Disaster Response Force — 16 battalions, ~18,000 personnel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — NDRF (Force) Operational Role (2.5 Marks):**
- Recruited from **BSF, CRPF, ITBP, CISF, SSB, Assam Rifles**; **3-week basic + 5-week advanced training**.
- Equipped with **collapsible boats, life-jackets, CBRN equipment, sniffer dogs**.
- **Pre-positioned** in 16 locations across India based on disaster vulnerability map.
- Standard response time: **< 30 minutes** after deployment order.

**Step 3 — SDRF / NDRF (Funds) Mechanics (1.5 Marks):**
- SDRF covers **localized disasters** within state list (cyclone, flood, drought, fire, earthquake, hailstorm, landslides, cloudburst, pest attack, cold wave, heat wave).
- NDRF Fund released by **High-Level Committee (HLC)** chaired by Union Home Secretary when state capacity is overwhelmed.

**Step 4 — Critical Evaluation (1 Mark):**
*Limitation:* SDRF ceilings are often outdated and do not account for inflation or climate-induced disaster escalation. The 15th Finance Commission recommended **revising the cost norms** for SDRF.

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (b):** *[7 Marks]*

**Step 1 — UN Cluster System (3 Marks):**
The **Cluster Approach** was adopted by **UN-OCHA in 2005** (post-Indian Ocean Tsunami lessons) to improve predictability, accountability, and partnership in humanitarian response.

**12 Global Clusters:**
1. **Agriculture (FAO)**
2. **Camp Coordination / Camp Management (IOM/UNHCR)**
3. **Early Recovery (UNDP)**
4. **Education (UNICEF)**
5. **Emergency Shelter (IFRC/UNHCR)**
6. **Food Security (WFP/FAO)**
7. **Health (WHO)**
8. **Logistics (WFP)**
9. **Nutrition (UNICEF)**
10. **Protection (UNHCR/UNICEF)**
11. **WASH (UNICEF)**
12. **Telecommunications (WFP/UNICEF)**

**Step 2 — Cross-Border / Large-Scale Response Mechanism (2 Marks):**
- **UN-OCHA Flash Appeal** — issued within 72 hours; target: $100m–$1b+ depending on scale.
- **CERF (Central Emergency Response Fund)** — $500m–$1b annual capacity for rapid funding.
- **INSARAG** coordinates international USAR teams (2015 Nepal Earthquake: 67 USAR teams from 32 countries).

**Step 3 — Case Study: 2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami (2 Marks):**
- Indian government's effective coordination resulted in **75,000 lives saved in Sri Lanka**.
- **$13.6 billion** global humanitarian assistance mobilized.
- Lesson: Sovereign government + international solidarity = **better outcome** than either alone.
- Lesson Learned: **early warning systems (IOTWS)** became a global public good — funded by World Bank and USAID.

**Step 4 — Concluding Statement:**
Cross-border disasters demand a **dual sovereignty-respect model** — UN Clusters operate *at the request of* and *in coordination with* the affected national government. The Sendai Framework reinforces this through its Priority 2 (*"Strengthen disaster risk governance to enhance coordination across institutional levels and sectors"*).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose 2–3 Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SDRF (Fund) with NDRF (Force).** Always clarify context.
> 2. **Writing "UN is the apex body"** — UN-OCHA is a *coordinator*, not a *command* body. National sovereignty remains paramount.
> 3. **Ignoring NDMA's *Guidelines*** — Examiners reward specific references (e.g., NDMA's Cyclone Guidelines 2018, COVID-19 Guidelines).
> 4. **Not mentioning the Sphere Standards or Code of Conduct** for any 7-mark NGO question.
> 5. **Omitting case studies** — Always include at least one Indian case (Kerala 2018, Uttarakhand 2013, Cyclone Phailin 2013, COVID-19).
> 6. **Forgetting the temporal sequencing** of response (0–72 hours, 3–15 days, 15+ days).
> 7. **Ignoring the Sendai Framework's four priorities** in any question about international cooperation.
> 8. **Treating NGOs as a homogenous group** — distinguish operational, advocacy, and community-based organizations.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Recall List — Print This and Memorize Before Exam**

**Government Bodies:**
- DM Act 2005 (Sections 3, 14, 25, 56 for NDMA/SDMA/DDMA/Local)
- 4-tier: National → State → District → Local
- **NDMA** (PM Chair) = apex policy; **NDRF** (16 battalions) = apex response
- **SDRF Fund** = 75:25 Centre:State; **NDRF Fund** = 100% Centre

**International Bodies:**
- **UN-OCHA** = coordinator (not commander)
- **Cluster Approach** = 12 sectors, agency-led
- **Red Cross:** ICRC (conflict) + IFRC (disasters) + National Societies
- **IFRC Sphere Standards** + **Code of Conduct 1994**
- **Frameworks:** Yokohama 1994 → Hyogo 2005 → Sendai 2015–2030
- **Finance:** World Bank, ADB, CERF, CBPF

**NGO Bodies:**
- **Functions:** preparedness → response → recovery → mitigation → advocacy
- **4 Humanitarian Principles:** Humanity, Impartiality, Neutrality, Independence
- **Famous NGOs:** MSF, Oxfam, Goonj, SEWA, BRAC, Save the Children
- **Indian law:** NGOs registered under Societies Registration Act 1860, Indian Trusts Act 1882, or Section 8 of Companies Act 2013

**High-Yield Concepts:**
- 3C Framework (Coordination, Cooperation, Complementarity)
- Build Back Better (Sendai Priority 4)
- All-of-Society engagement (Sendai)
- District = most critical tier (DDMA)
- Last-mile delivery = NGO forte

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychological Response and Management (Trauma, Stress, Rumor and Panic)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT744 - DISASTER PREPAREDNESS AND EMERGENCY PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Psychological Response and Management (Trauma, Stress, Rumor and Panic)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

### Trauma
